ci: Stories are shared in different ways.
lo: Imagining stories together; Ways stories are shared; Connecting with stories

s.d: Students create and perform their own stories by choosing characters, settings, and props within Puppet Pals, bringing familiar or original tales to life through digital puppetry. They record their performances to share with classmates, exploring how stories can be expressed through voice and movement in a fun, interactive way that supports imagination and narrative skills.

Students become Mystery Storytellers by recording their own imaginative audio stories that explore the central idea 'Stories are shared in different ways.' Using Adobe Express Podcasting, they add sound effects and music to bring their narratives to life, developing oral language skills and creative expression in a fun, accessible format. Teachers can share these podcasts with families to celebrate each child's unique voice and storytelling style. https://podcast.adobe.com

Students bring their favourite story characters to life by taking photos or drawings and using ChatterPix Kids to record their voices telling a part of the story. This activity helps Prep learners explore how stories are shared through voice and animation, encouraging creativity and oral storytelling as they imagine and express stories together.

Students document their story-creation process by capturing photos and short videos of their developmental play (using costumes, puppets, Lego, etc.) and uploading them to Seesaw. They can add voice reflections about their stories, allowing families to see and hear the diversity of storytelling in the classroom.

Students illustrate their own imaginative story scenes inspired by the central idea that stories are shared in different ways, using Sketchbook?s simple drawing tools to bring characters and settings to life. As they experiment with colours, shapes, and textures, learners express emotions and narrative moments visually, creating unique storyboards that capture the mood and flow of their favourite tales.

Students work in small groups to invent and build a large, interactive 'story map' using cardboard, fabric scraps, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each map represents a setting from a familiar or original story (e.g., a forest, castle, or outback). Students design and decorate their map with 3D features like trees, rivers, or caves. Next, they program a Bee-Bot or Sphero Indi to travel along a path that follows the sequence of the story. At each stop, students place handmade props, puppets, or character models and record a short audio clip (using iPads) of themselves narrating that part of the story. As the robot visits each scene, students trigger the audio and retell the story, blending hands-on making, digital storytelling, and coding in a multimodal, collaborative experience.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Stories are shared in different ways' through imaginative play, daily storytelling, retelling familiar stories, and hands-on activities using props, costumes, puppets, and digital tools. The unit emphasizes student agency, creativity, and multimodal expression, including a Mystery Storyteller program, narrative writing, and an Indigenous storytelling excursion.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Stories are shared in different ways.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Imagining stories together; Ways stories are shared; Connecting with stories

KEY CONCEPTS: Responsibility, form

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Showing a mystery box (with costumes inside) as provocation; exploring imaginative story creation during developmental play sessions using finger puppets, dress ups, and sequencing story games; during literacy lessons each week, retelling stories and reading different story books; starting "mystery storytellers" in Week 2.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: Exploring imaginative story creation with finger puppets, dress ups, and sequencing story games; retelling stories during literacy lessons; reading different story books; "mystery storytellers" activity in Week 2; PSPE activities where athletics events are introduced through imaginative contexts, encouraging students to connect movement to ideas and feelings; group activities like relays and team games to show that stories can be shared through actions, teamwork, and play.

ASSESSMENT: Not specified.

TEACHER NOTES: Not specified.

ci: Everyone has a role to play in the community.
lo: My communities; Contributing to the local community; Connections in my community

s.d: Learners create digital puppet shows in Puppet Pals, role-playing different community helpers and school staff to demonstrate their understanding of roles and responsibilities. Students record their voices and animate characters, sharing their collaborative stories with the class to celebrate the diversity of their school community.

Students take photos of staff members or community helpers (with permission) or draw their own, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short voice messages about each person's role in the community, fostering understanding of diverse contributions.

Students create a digital portfolio on Seesaw where each child uploads a drawing or photo of a community helper they have learned about, records a short voice message explaining the helper?s role, and shares why that role is important to their community. Teachers guide students to collaborate by commenting and encouraging each other?s contributions, building a vibrant class collection that celebrates everyone?s role in the community.

Students create simple animated stories in ScratchJR where characters take on different community roles, demonstrating how everyone contributes to the school community. Learners program their characters to move between places like the library, classroom, and office, narrating their role and why it is important, reinforcing the central idea through interactive storytelling.

Guide students to create vibrant visual collages in PicCollage representing the different roles and connections in their school community, using photos, stickers, and drawings. These can be printed or shared digitally to celebrate community diversity.

Students work in small groups to select a community helper role within the school (e.g., gardener, librarian, crossing supervisor). Each group designs and builds a physical model of their chosen helper using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, ensuring their creation includes key tools or symbols of that role. Next, students integrate a simple programmable robot (such as a Bee-Bot or Sphero Indi) by designing and constructing a themed obstacle course or 'work zone' for their helper. They program the robot to perform a task that matches their helper's job (e.g., Bee-Bot 'delivers books' to the library, 'waters' plants in the garden, or 'helps' students cross a road made from painted cardboard). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise by interviewing real staff, define their helper's tasks, ideate and sketch designs, build prototypes, test and iterate their models and robot pathways, then share their creations and learning with the class.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Everyone has a role to play in the community' by investigating roles within their school, creating wonderings, interviewing staff, and sharing their learning through videos and presentations. Activities include guided walks, role-play, collaborative projects, and reflecting on how individuals contribute to the community.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Everyone has a role to play in the community.

LINES OF INQUIRY: My communities; Contributing to the local community; Connections in my community

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Week 1 ? Photos of staff in the school: what is the role of these people? Students create drawings of each person. Week 2 ? Photos of staff in the school: creating wonderings about these people.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Read picture story books about schools (e.g., Whose School Is This?)
- Class discussion: Who helps make our school a great place?
- Create a mind map of people we know in our school community
- Walk around the school to observe different areas and people
- Read stories about teachers and their roles; shared oral discussion: "What does a teacher do all day?"
- Draw and label what we think a teacher's job involves
- Wonder Wall: Add questions about teachers and other staff
- Guided school tour; discuss the purpose of different areas (library, office, canteen, etc.)
- Add wonderings to the Wonder Wall: "Who works here? What do they do?"
- Choose key staff (principal, librarian, office staff, cleaner, groundskeeper); brainstorm and record questions to ask them
- Role-play: "What if I were the principal?"
- In small groups, visit and interview different staff members; practice taking turns asking questions and listening; reflect and share what was learned and what surprised them
- Present learning to the school at assembly

ASSESSMENT: 
- Students created their own video to share with the school community after creating their own wonderings
- Assembly presentation to communicate understanding
- Evidence includes participation in interviews, video creation, and oral presentations

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong emphasis on inquiry-based, experiential, and reflective strategies
- Wonder wall activities support student-driven questioning and responsive learning
- Collaboration with specialists (Music: matching sounds to staff roles; PE: group cooperation and teamwork; Digital: video recording with Mr Holt)
- Opportunities for agency: students choose how to represent learning (video, drawing, oral)
- Transdisciplinary connections: literacy (vocabulary, oral language), digital technology/media (video production), social capability (empathy, appreciation for others' roles)
- Action opportunities: fostering a sense of belonging, responsibility, and agency within the school community
- Teacher reflection notes highlight the value of authentic experiences, real audiences, and scaffolding for student confidence and participation

ci: Scientists follow a method to discover new things.
lo: The scientific process; Observing my world; Drawing conclusions

s.d: Students become 'junior scientists' by using Explain Everything to visually document each step of a real science experiment (e.g., ice melting, ramp rolling). They draw predictions, record observations with the iPad camera, and narrate their thinking, creating a digital 'science method story' that can be replayed and shared with families.

Students use Seesaw to capture photos and voice recordings as they observe, predict, and test during hands-on science activities. Each child uploads a sequence of images and audio reflections, showing their understanding of the scientific process in a highly visual, accessible way.

Students use Canva's drag-and-drop features to create simple, illustrated posters showing the steps of a science investigation (e.g., 'First, we predict... Next, we test...'). They can use the 'Remove Background' tool to add themselves into their science scenes, making the process personal and engaging.

Students collaboratively build a digital class book documenting their science experiments. Each page features a different step of the scientific method, with students adding their own photos, drawings, and voice explanations to demonstrate their learning.

Students use the iPad Camera to take close-up photos of their science observations (e.g., melting ice, weather changes, sorting materials). These images are then sequenced to tell the story of their experiment, supporting visual recall and oral language development.

Prep students use the Design Cycle to invent and build their own 'Weather Wonder Machines' that can demonstrate or measure a weather phenomenon. In groups, students brainstorm and sketch ideas (e.g., a rain gauge, wind spinner, or simple sun tracker), then construct their machines using recycled materials, cardboard, plastic bottles, and craft supplies. They integrate Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot robots by designing their machines to interact with the robot?such as having the robot trigger a spinning windmill or drive through a rain gauge tunnel. Students test, observe, and improve their machines, recording results with drawings and oral reflections. The project blends hands-on building, early robotics, and scientific inquiry, letting students experience how scientists invent tools to investigate the world.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea that scientists follow a method to discover new things. Key activities include nature walks for observation, weather tracking, hands-on experiments (like ice melting and ramp rolling), and sorting materials by properties. The scientific process is made explicit through repeated cycles of predicting, testing, observing, recording, and sharing findings, with a strong emphasis on drawing, oral reflection, and collaborative inquiry.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Scientists follow a method to discover new things.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The scientific process; Observing my world; Drawing conclusions

KEY CONCEPTS: Perspective, connection, form

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Video - Scientist's role (Week 1-3): As a class, brainstorming what a scientist does, what sort of things a scientist works with, and what things a scientist can discover. Exploring picture story books about rock collections, nature, and parks.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Nature Detective Walk (Week 4): Read 'The Tiny Explorers', go outside with magnifying glasses and clipboards to observe plants, insects, and other living things, record findings with drawings and writing, sort and group creatures or plants by features.
- Weather Watchers (Week 5): Daily weather observations, drawing a "weather picture" each morning, sequencing pictures, discussing patterns, comparing with weather in another place via webcam or online weather reports.
- Ice Melting Challenge (Week 6): Groups receive ice cubes and tools (bowl, spoon, salt, warm water), predict and test fastest ways to melt ice, record results in a chart.
- Rolling Ramp Investigation (Week 7): Use ramps and objects (balls, toy cars, cylinders), predict which will roll farthest, test and measure, discuss factors affecting movement.
- Mystery Materials Bag (Week 8): Explore properties of materials (fabric, plastic, metal, sponge, wood) by touch, describe and identify, discuss uses.

ASSESSMENT: Student work samples (observation drawings, weather chart, written responses), sharing at Student Led conferences about the nature walk experience, class discussions, and presentations of findings.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include building on prior learning and supporting student-initiated action, such as participation, lifestyle choices, and advocacy.
- Chart of a Scientist on display to capture initial thinking.
- Transdisciplinary connections: Written responses in English, science experiments, nature walk experience, music (exploring rhythm patterns and instrumental timbres from past and present), and opportunities for digital skills integration (e.g., using webcams for weather comparison).
- Outdoor and sensory learning are emphasized to boost engagement and support different learning styles.
- Opportunities for students to receive teacher and peer feedback are embedded throughout the unit.
- To improve: involve students more in self-assessment and reflection to increase awareness of their own learning process.

lo: The different living things around us; The roles living things play in nature; How humans and other living things share space

ci: Relationships impact our wellbeing.
lo: Developing relationships; How those around us impact our behaviours and interactions; Keeping ourselves healthy and safe

s.d: Students create and perform short puppet shows using Puppet Pals to act out friendship scenarios that demonstrate how relationships impact wellbeing. They design characters and settings that show sharing, helping, and caring, then record their voices to bring their stories to life, highlighting positive behaviours and emotions in friendships.

Students compose and record original soundtracks in GarageBand to represent different emotions and relationship scenarios. They experiment with tempo, instruments, and sound effects to express feelings like happiness, frustration, or calm, then share their audio creations as part of class discussions about wellbeing and friendship.

Students create a digital portfolio on Seesaw where they draw pictures of themselves with friends and record short voice messages explaining how these relationships make them feel and why they are important for their wellbeing. Teachers guide learners to share and listen to each other's stories, fostering a supportive classroom community that reflects the unit?s central idea about relationships impacting wellbeing.

Students create vibrant visual collages in PicCollage that illustrate 'What friendship looks like at school,' using photos, stickers, and emojis to represent acts of kindness, teamwork, and support. They build their collages to reflect the unit?s central idea that relationships impact our wellbeing, explicitly connecting to developing friendships and how those around us influence our behaviours. Learners then present their collages to the class as conversation starters, fostering discussion about positive interactions and keeping ourselves healthy and safe.

Guide students to code simple animations in ScratchJr that show two characters navigating a playground scenario?such as sharing, resolving a conflict, or inviting someone to play?demonstrating how positive relationships impact wellbeing. Students can narrate their stories and share them with peers.

Students work in small groups to design and build a 'Friendship Fort' using large cardboard boxes, fabric, recycled materials, and popsticks. The fort represents a safe space where positive relationships and emotions are celebrated. Each group uses an iPad with the ChatterPix Kids app to create digital 'emotion faces'?photos of their own faces decorated and voiced with messages about friendship and wellbeing. These digital faces are printed and physically attached to the fort walls alongside hand-drawn emotion art. The design cycle is followed: students empathise by discussing what makes them feel safe and happy with friends, define features their fort needs, ideate creative designs, prototype with materials, and test by role-playing friendship scenarios inside the fort. The final fort becomes a collaborative classroom installation, blending digital and physical creations to explore how relationships shape wellbeing.

plannerText: The Prep 'Who We Are' unit explores how relationships impact wellbeing, focusing on developing friendships, understanding how those around us influence our behaviours, and recognising balance in our interactions. Key activities include read-alouds, oral discussions, drawing emotions, role-play, and creating an essential agreement to foster positive classroom relationships.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Relationships impact our wellbeing.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Developing relationships; How those around us impact our behaviours and interactions; Recognising our balance

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, causation, change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Read alouds that explore the lines of inquiry, followed by oral discussions and brainstorming as a whole class in response. Specific activities include creating an Essential Agreement as a class, and reading "How Do Dinosaurs Play with Their Friends?" by Jane Yolen and Mark Teague, with students discussing and sharing what makes a good friend.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Essential Agreement creation as a class
- Read alouds: "How Do Dinosaurs Play with Their Friends?" (Jane Yolen & Mark Teague), "The Feelings Book" (Todd Parr), "Have You Filled a Bucket Today?" (Carol McCloud), "Should I Share My Ice Cream?" (Mo Willems)
- Oral discussions about friendship, feelings, and kindness
- Drawing activities: students draw how a friend can support them when feeling different emotions
- Pair work: practicing sharing and taking turns with materials (e.g., building a tower with blocks)
- Activity: Creating actions through photo form to represent Learner Profile attributes
- Visual Art: Self-portraits focusing on identity and emotions, drawing faces with different emotions, responding to music with artwork expressing emotions
- PSPE: Activities focused on cooperation, emotional expression, body awareness, personal space, and turn-taking in PE
- Portfolio task reflecting on play, enjoyment, and movement's effect on body and emotions

ASSESSMENT: 
- Portfolio task where students reflect on what they enjoy, how they play with others, and how movement affects their body and emotions
- Evidence from group games, role-play, drawings, and oral discussions
- Ongoing teacher observation of social skills and application of learning in classroom and play situations

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Local connections focus on students transitioning to school life and navigating classroom/playground dynamics
- Oral discussions used to assess prior knowledge about positive relationships and friendship
- Collaboration with specialists (e.g., PE, Visual Art) is noted as an area for further development
- Visual Art and PSPE are integrated to support identity, emotions, and wellbeing
- Technology: Students could use digital tools to document their learning (e.g., photos of actions representing Learner Profile attributes, digital portfolios)
- Adjustments made for class size and dynamics; activities adapted to student needs and readiness
- Emphasis on student agency: co-constructing Essential Agreement, choice in how to show learning (drawing, role-play, discussion)
- The unit is designed to support transition to school, build friendships, and establish classroom expectations for a positive learning environment

ci: Emotions can be expressed in different ways.
lo: Recognising our own and others' emotions; Responding to and interpreting our emotions; Different ways we express emotions

s.d: Students photograph their own facial expressions or create drawings of different emotions, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images with their own voice recordings, expressing how they feel and why. This brings emotions to life and encourages students to reflect on and share their feelings in a playful, creative way.

Students use Canva's visual design tools to create digital emotion posters, combining expressive colors, shapes, and the 'Remove Background' feature to collage their own photos or artwork. They can visually represent different emotions and add simple text or icons to communicate feelings, supporting visual literacy and emotional vocabulary.

Students collaboratively author a digital class book about emotions, each contributing a page with a drawing, photo, or collage representing a feeling, and recording a short audio explanation. The final book can be shared with families or read aloud in class, celebrating the diversity of emotional expression.

Students document their emotional learning journey by uploading photos, drawings, or voice recordings to Seesaw. Teachers can prompt students to reflect on how they expressed emotions during different activities, and families can respond with supportive comments, building a home-school connection around emotional literacy.

Students use Adobe Express to create short, vibrant digital posters or slideshows that visually represent a range of emotions using color, imagery, and simple animation. They can record their voices describing a time they felt a certain way, integrating visual and oral storytelling.

Students work in teams to design and build a large, interactive 'emotion adventure board' using recycled cardboard, felt, popsticks, and craft materials. Each pathway on the board represents a different emotion (e.g., happy, sad, surprised), with physical obstacles, tunnels, or bridges that symbolize how we move through feelings. Students decorate each section with expressive artwork and emotion symbols. Using Sphero Indi cars, students program simple color-coded routes for the cars to travel along the pathways. As the Indi moves, it stops at each emotion zone, prompting students to act out, discuss, or show with their faces and bodies how that emotion feels. This project combines collaborative building, creative expression, and basic coding to help students physically and digitally explore how we express emotions.

plannerText: The unit 'How We Express Ourselves' explores how emotions can be expressed in different ways. Students engage in activities such as reading picture books about emotions, creating artworks and masks to represent feelings, participating in role-play and group discussions, and using visual and verbal communication to recognise, interpret, and communicate emotions. The unit emphasizes creativity, self-awareness, and social-emotional learning through a variety of expressive modes.

First, they brainstorm and sketch how to represent different feelings (happy, sad, excited, etc.) through facial features, colors, and accessories. Then, they use cardboard, recycled materials, felt, googly eyes, and craft supplies to physically build and decorate 3D robot 'faces' and bodies that attach securely to the Sphero Indi. Each team codes simple color paths for their robot to follow, choreographing a 'parade' that expresses a sequence of emotions (e.g., Indi moves along a blue path for 'sad', then a yellow path for 'happy'). The project culminates in a class robot parade, with students explaining their design choices and how their robots' movements and appearance express different emotions, connecting hands-on making, coding, and social-emotional learning.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Emotions can be expressed in different ways.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Recognising our own and others' emotions; Responding to and interpreting our emotions; Communicating our emotions

KEY CONCEPTS: Perspective, causation

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Write the word 'feelings' on a big classroom chart. Use think, pair, share to build instructional routine for sharing thinking. Students turn and talk about the word feelings and what it means with a talking buddy. Teacher records ideas heard as students talk with a partner. Students work in four groups to record their thinking about feelings using words, drawings or sentences. Questions from this activity are added to the JS Wonder Wall.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Read aloud and discuss picture story books connecting to emotions (e.g., You are Brave by Margaret O'Hair, I am Peace by Susan Verde, In My Heart by Jo Witek).
- Shared reading comprehension using "My Many Coloured Days" with visualising and drawing thoughts as the text is read.
- Self-assessment rubric and See, Think, Wonder routines with feelings faces.
- Creative activity: My Many Coloured Days ? creative feelings people.
- Group collage depicting an emotion, including a photograph of the group showing this emotion, focusing on colour associations.
- Individual mask-making to show an emotion, with group guessing games.
- Role-play activities to express emotions through body language and facial expressions.
- Music: Exploring how music can express emotions (tempo, pitch, timbre, dynamics) and creating a soundscape to accompany a book.
- PSPE: Health lessons on recognising and communicating emotions respectfully; PE Teamwork Challenge to express feelings in collaborative settings.
- Wellbeing and Social Thinking curriculum connections.
- Ongoing use of the JS Wonder Wall for student questions and wonderings.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Self-assessment rubric (T1, W9)
- See, Think, Wonder charts
- Creative feelings people activity
- Group and individual art projects (collage, masks)
- Student participation in discussions and role-plays
- Portfolio tasks (e.g., Communicating Feelings health task, PE Teamwork Challenge)
- Music soundscape recordings (noted as not included in final book recording)
- Sharing learning with the community

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Wellbeing, Social Thinking, and Health curriculum.
- Transdisciplinary connections with English (literature and vocabulary), Visual Art (colour and emotion), Music (soundscapes), and PSPE (emotional regulation in health and PE).
- Opportunities for student agency through choice in art and reflection activities.
- Use of digital tools not specified, but opportunities exist for digital portfolios (e.g., SeeSaw), recording soundscapes, and documenting learning with photographs.
- Teacher reflection notes highlight the value of peer reflection, structured self-assessment, and the importance of integrating emotional literacy across subjects.
- Next steps include more structured peer reflection and ensuring music recordings are included in final presentations.

ci: Services are established to help the needs of people in the community.
lo: Local services; Why we need local services in our community; How services meet personal and community needs

s.d: Students take photos of themselves or drawings of community helpers, then use ChatterPix Kids to record their voices as if they are the helper explaining their job and how they help the community. This creates a digital 'living museum' of local services, making learning interactive and personal.

Students create a collaborative digital book about local community services. Each student authors a page featuring a chosen community helper, adding photos, drawings, and voice recordings to explain the service and its importance. The final book can be shared with families and the wider school community.

Using Canva's visual templates and 'Remove Background' feature, students design posters celebrating different community helpers. They can combine their own photos (taken with the iPad camera) and illustrations, then add simple labels or speech bubbles to highlight how each service meets community needs.

Students document their learning journey by recording short videos or audio reflections about the services they encounter during a 'community walk' around the school. They upload photos and voice notes to Seesaw, creating a digital portfolio that captures their understanding of how services support the community.

Students use Epic to research and listen to read-alouds about community helpers. They then create a simple fact file or digital collage about a chosen service, using visuals and minimal text to demonstrate their understanding of how that service meets people's needs.

Students work in teams to design and build a model 'community' using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies, including key locations such as a hospital, fire station, and post office. Each team selects a community helper and invents a 'rescue mission' scenario (e.g., delivering medicine, helping someone in trouble). Students then craft simple rescue vehicles or helper robots using Bee-Bots or Sphero Indi. They physically construct obstacle courses and pathways in their model city, integrating ramps, bridges, and tunnels. Students program their robots to navigate the city, simulating how community helpers respond to emergencies and provide services. The project culminates with students presenting their missions, explaining their design choices, and reflecting on how their creations represent real-life community services.

plannerText: Students explored the central idea 'Services are established to help the needs of people in the community' through read-alouds, guest speakers (such as the Royal Flying Doctor Service), class discussions, and student-led presentations about community helpers. Activities included writing thank-you letters, creating reports and presentations, and engaging with digital research and reflection tasks.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Services are established to help the needs of people in the community.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Local services; Why we need local services in our community; How services meet personal and local community needs

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, causation, function

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Moving to Mars! If we started a new community on Mars, what services would we need? Students discuss and record their wonderings on a Wonder Wall. Students work as a class to design a community on butcher's paper.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Students complete a mini-book about community helpers.
- Carousel activity: students record their ideas about needs, wants, services, and goods; unpack what a service is.
- Guest speaker visits: Nurse Simone and a doctor (cardiologist, parent).
- School community interviews: students create interview questions for local community service people.
- Learning walk: exploring the school community and its services.
- Ongoing reading lessons with community helper texts, vocabulary building, and comprehension.
- Incursion with the Royal Flying Doctor Service.
- Students choose a community helper to write an information report about.
- Home learning: students prepare a presentation about a chosen community helper and present findings to the class.
- Students write a letter to local 'heroes' (community workers).
- Use of digital resources: Get Epic! for research, PowerPoint presentations for viewing and presenting tasks.
- Library sessions focusing on services such as Libraries, Outback services, Surf Lifesavers, Service Animals, supported by specific PowerPoint resources and books.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Anecdotal assessment and observation of student participation and wonderings.
- Student presentations about a chosen community helper.
- Community helper information report.
- Letters to local community workers.
- SeeSaw task for sharing learning with the community.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with literacy (reading nonfiction texts, writing reports, presentations).
- Collaboration with Library for aligned read-alouds and resource support.
- Use of digital tools (Get Epic!, PowerPoint, SeeSaw) for research, presentation, and portfolio documentation.
- Guest speakers and real-world connections significantly enhance engagement.
- Opportunities for student agency through choice of community helper and presentation format.
- Future suggestions: incorporate more hands-on or role-playing experiences, and provide more structured support for formulating questions and presentations.
- Transdisciplinary connections with social studies, literacy, and inquiry skills; some integration with Chinese (research and presentation skills).
- Assessment includes both formative (anecdotal, observation) and summative (presentations, reports, letters) evidence.

ci: Patterns help us to interpret the world around us.
lo: Patterns and cycles in our lives; Different patterns around us; How patterns can be created, interpreted and extended

s.d: Students photograph patterns they find in nature or around the school using the iPad camera, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the patterns and record a short explanation of what the pattern is and how it helps us interpret the world. This brings patterns to life and encourages oral language development.

Students create a visual collage of different patterns and cycles (e.g., butterfly life cycle, seasons, water cycle) using Canva's drag-and-drop interface. They can use the 'Remove Background' feature to layer their own photos of patterns and add simple labels or icons, making connections between visual patterns and real-world examples.

Students collaboratively build a digital class book documenting patterns and cycles they observe throughout the unit. Each student contributes a page with a drawing, photo, or voice recording describing a pattern (e.g., leaf shapes, brickwork, animal life cycles), supporting multimodal expression and early literacy.

Students use Seesaw to capture and share their investigations of patterns in their environment. They can upload photos, draw over them to highlight repeating elements, and record their thinking. Teachers and peers can comment, fostering reflection and dialogue about patterns and cycles.

Students use the Epic app to research and explore interactive books about patterns in nature, animal life cycles, and seasons. They can listen to read-alouds and use the app's built-in quizzes to reinforce understanding, supporting independent inquiry and digital research skills.

Students work in teams to design and build a large, tactile 'Seasons Garden' using recycled cardboard, fabric scraps, clay, and natural materials. Each group creates a section representing a different season, crafting trees, plants, and weather features that change through the year. Using Sphero Indi cars, students program simple color-coded paths through the garden to simulate the journey of a raindrop, animal, or seed as it experiences seasonal changes. The physical garden is interactive: movable parts (e.g., leaves, snow, flowers) allow students to demonstrate and discuss patterns and cycles, while the Sphero Indi's journey helps them visualize and narrate these changes in real time.

plannerText: The Year 1 'How the World Works' unit focuses on exploring patterns and cycles in the natural world, such as life cycles, seasons, and the water cycle. Students research, observe, and document patterns using digital tools, engage in hands-on investigations, and create models to interpret and extend their understanding of how patterns help us make sense of our environment.

Teams use iPads to record short videos explaining the patterns and cycles demonstrated in their interactive model, blending creative building, basic robotics, and digital storytelling.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Patterns helps us to interpret the world around us.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Patterns and cycles in our lives; Different patterns around us; How patterns can be created, interpreted and extended

KEY CONCEPTS: Change, function, perspective

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Provocation of unveiling the butterfly chrysalis ? students are asked what they think is underneath the cloth, if they can think of another animal that changes, and what they think will happen to the chrysalis. Chrysalis kit is placed in the classroom for 3 weeks, and each morning students observe changes.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Research skills using Get Epic to choose a life cycle of interest and demonstrate understanding in writing
- Technology use: iPad and Get Epic app for research
- Read aloud texts exploring information and vocabulary about different cycles
- Paraphrasing information about animal life-cycles using templates
- Exploring different types of patterns and cycles (animal life cycles, plant life-cycles, water cycle, seasons) through texts and videos
- Home learning task: students choose and research a cycle of personal interest
- Local exploration: taking photos of objects with magnifying glass lens iPad and observing patterns in the local community
- Visual Art: drawing patterns found in nature and buildings
- Design Technologies: making a model of a butterfly's lifecycle
- Digital Portfolio: SeeSaw
- Music: Watching videos about different life-cycles (YouTube Channel ? Pants Bear)
- Chinese: Learning patterns in the days of the week through "The Very Hungry Caterpillar" story and narrating favourite parts in Chinese

ASSESSMENT: 
- Written responses to research tasks
- Paraphrasing information using templates
- Home learning research project and presentation
- Digital Portfolio (SeeSaw)
- Observations and anecdotal assessment of participation and understanding
- Butterfly journal observing changes to chrysalises

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include participation, lifestyle choices, and advocacy (e.g., UN Sustainable Development Goals)
- Transdisciplinary connections: Literacy (read alouds, writing), Maths (measurement ? time, seasons), Science (posing questions, making observations, comparing predictions), Social Studies (collecting and recording information), Visual Art, PE (emotional development linked to perspective and change), Chinese (patterns in language)
- Digital skills: Use of Epic for research, SeeSaw for digital portfolio, iPad for observation and documentation
- Student agency supported through choice in research topics and home learning tasks
- Integration with Australian Curriculum and cross-curriculum priorities (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Histories and Cultures, Asia and Australia's Engagement with Asia, Sustainability)
- Opportunities for further exploration in Art and Maths patterns noted for future units
- Students particularly enjoyed hands-on experiences (chrysalis kit) and expressed interest in observing other animal life-cycles (e.g., chicks hatching)

ci: Plants are a life sustaining resource.
lo: Products derived from plants; Plants all around us; The interconnectedness of plants

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Homes reflect our culture and environment.
lo: Different types of homes; Characteristics of a home; The importance of sustainable living spaces

s.d: Students select or draw a picture of a unique home from around the world, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the home and record a voiceover explaining its features, materials, and how it reflects the local culture and environment. This brings global perspectives to life in a playful, memorable way.

Students create a visual Venn diagram in Canva comparing two types of homes (e.g., igloo vs. yurt), using images, icons, and minimal text to highlight similarities and differences in materials, climate adaptation, and cultural significance.

Each student authors a page in a collaborative digital class book using Book Creator, showcasing a different type of home. They add photos, drawings, and voice recordings to describe what makes each home special and how it connects to its environment.

Students document their learning journey by uploading photos of their mini home builds, drawing diagrams, and recording short reflections about how their chosen home reflects culture and environment. Families can comment and ask questions to extend learning.

Students use Epic to research and gather facts about homes from different cultures, then create a simple fact file or digital poster with images and key points, supporting their understanding with rich, age-appropriate nonfiction texts.

Students work in small groups to research a type of home from a different culture or environment (e.g., igloos, stilt houses, yurts). Using recycled materials, cardboard, fabric, and natural items, each group physically builds a detailed model of their chosen home, focusing on features that reflect its culture and environmental adaptations. Then, students use Bee-Bots or Sphero Indi robots, decorating them as 'global visitors.' They collaboratively design and lay out a large village map on the floor, placing their model homes in context (e.g., near rivers, forests, deserts). Students program their robots to travel between homes, sharing recorded audio facts or stories (using simple voice recorders or iPad voice memos attached to the robots) about each home's unique features and sustainability ideas. This fully hands-on project integrates digital programming, creative construction, and cultural exploration.

plannerText: Students explore how homes reflect culture and environment by investigating different types of homes from around the world, comparing features, and discussing sustainability. Activities include read-alouds, research using Epic, Venn diagrams, building mini homes, and a learning showcase where students present their creations and findings.

This hands-on project integrates design, building, basic robotics, and cultural storytelling, culminating in an interactive showcase where peers and families can explore the village and learn about global diversity.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Homes reflect our culture and environment.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Different types of homes; Characteristics of a home; The importance of sustainability

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection and Form

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students use craft materials to make a classroom display of houses. Writing task: Write what you love about your home. Read Epic texts "Homes Around the World" and "Homes Then and Now".

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Reading information texts about different types of homes (e.g., Gunya, Queenslander, houseboat, igloo, yurt, apartment, etc.)
- Watching informative videos about different types of homes
- Venn diagram comparing two types of homes
- Building mini houses (Design Technologies)
- Observational drawings of houses from around the world in Visual Art; select a house and create it using different mediums
- Exploring Epic to research facts about different types of houses
- Writing tasks: paraphrasing facts, Venn diagrams, and writing about what students love about their own home
- Learning Showcase where students present their mini homes and learning
- Dance & Drama: Exploring connection and form through homes and environments, discovering non-human personifications
- Chinese: Learning about Chinese characters, comparing past (oracle bone script) and modern forms

ASSESSMENT: Building mini homes; Learning Showcase; Writing tasks (what do you love about your home, paraphrasing facts, Venn diagrams); Digital Portfolio ? SeeSaw

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include building mini homes and presenting at the Learning Showcase.
- Students are encouraged to research and present about homes from different cultures, supporting open-mindedness and international mindedness.
- Digital skills are integrated through research on Epic and documentation in SeeSaw.
- Design Technologies is used for building mini houses.
- Transdisciplinary connections are made with Social Studies (comparing past and present homes, considering sustainability), English (reading and writing tasks), Visual Art, and Chinese.
- The unit encourages student agency and creativity, with open-ended tasks and opportunities for students to choose how they present their learning (slides, videos, posters, etc.).
- Teacher reflection notes suggest that the open-ended nature of the home-building project fostered creativity and ownership, and that literacy was linked weekly in a transdisciplinary way. There is a suggestion to explore measurement in future iterations of the unit.

ci: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing.
lo: Safe environments; How our actions affect ourselves and others; Keeping our body and mind healthy

s.d: Students take photos of themselves making a 'healthy choice' (e.g., wearing a helmet, eating fruit, washing hands) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate their photo, recording a short message about how their action keeps them safe or supports wellbeing. This creates a fun, personal digital portfolio of safety and wellbeing actions.

Students collaboratively create a digital class book titled 'Our Healthy Choices,' with each student contributing a page featuring a drawing or photo of a safe or healthy action, a simple sentence, and an audio recording explaining their choice. The book can be shared with families and other classes to promote wellbeing.

Using Canva's visual templates and 'Remove Background' feature, students design vibrant posters showing safe and healthy habits (e.g., crossing the road safely, handwashing, healthy snacks). They can use the iPad camera to take photos and add stickers, icons, and their own drawings for a visually rich display.

Students document their daily wellbeing routines by taking photos or short videos (using the iPad camera) and uploading them to Seesaw. They add voice recordings to explain their actions, creating a digital wellbeing journal that teachers and families can view and comment on.

Students use the Epic app to explore age-appropriate books about the human body, safety, and healthy habits. After reading, they draw or act out one thing they learned and record a short reflection using the iPad camera, which is then shared on Seesaw or in class discussions.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, interactive board game that teaches about healthy routines. Using recycled cardboard, bottle caps, fabric scraps, and craft supplies, students create 3D spaces representing daily choices (e.g., brushing teeth, eating veggies, exercising, resting). They integrate Sphero Indi cars as game pieces: teams program the Indis with color tiles to follow different paths based on healthy or unhealthy choices. Along the way, students build physical obstacles and rewards (e.g., a popstick 'toothbrush bridge' or a clay 'fruit stand') that the Indi must navigate. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize by discussing daily routines, define healthy vs. unhealthy scenarios, ideate creative board elements, prototype physical spaces, test Indi navigation, and reflect on how their choices impact the game outcome and real-life wellbeing.

plannerText: Students explore how their actions influence safety and wellbeing through hands-on activities such as daily read-alouds about the human body, creating informative books on healthy habits, building life-size body models, and participating in inquiry stations focused on nutrition, exercise, hygiene, and rest. The unit emphasizes making healthy choices, reflecting on routines, and sharing learning with families through student-led conferences and digital portfolios.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing

LINES OF INQUIRY: How the human body grows and changes; Ways we can keep our body and mind healthy; Safe environments

KEY CONCEPTS: Function

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Class brainstorm and "body outline" activity?students label known parts on a blank human body template to elicit prior knowledge and curiosities about the human body. Follow-up includes developing wonder questions to guide the inquiry wall.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Daily interactive read-alouds of nonfiction texts (e.g., See Inside Your Body, The Human Body series, AI generated texts), with post-it notes for "new learning" added to the inquiry wall.
- Incursion: Mad About Science ? The Human Body, with hands-on exploration of the eye, respiratory system, and digestive system.
- Mini-lessons on nonfiction text features; students draft, illustrate, and publish their own Informative Book about the body and healthy habits, shared at Student-Led Conferences (SLCs).
- Healthy Habits Inquiry Stations: Nutrition sorting, exercise/heart rate exploration, hygiene role-play, sleep/rest charting.
- Life-Size Body Models: In pairs, students create organs and body systems, then assemble them on life-size body outlines.
- Life-Size Skeletons: Trace each child's body, introduce bones and the skeletal system, and assemble a life-size skeleton.
- Quiz Book for SLCs: Students generate quiz questions about body systems and healthy living, design a quiz book, and present at SLCs.
- Healthy Habits Presentations: Students create a presentation about their healthy habits using a multi-modal element (PowerPoint, booklet, poster, video, or model).
- Buddy Reading during Book Week: Students share their Human Body Book with Year 5s.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Informative writing (My Human Body Book) assessed for nonfiction text features and scientific understanding.
- Quiz Book for Student-Led Conferences as evidence of recall and application.
- Healthy Habits presentations (multi-modal).
- Anecdotal assessment during inquiry stations and hands-on activities.
- Sharing learning with families at SLCs and with Year 5 buddies.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with literacy (read-alouds, informative writing), science (body systems, health), and PE (physical development, safe movement choices).
- Inquiry wall and wonder wall used for student questions and new learning.
- Digital skills: Opportunity for guided use of digital resources or library texts for student-led research.
- Students encouraged to use multi-modal presentations (PowerPoint, video, poster, model).
- Transdisciplinary connections with Music (Vivaldi's Four Seasons for composition), Visual Art (not specified), and PE (movement, teamwork, wellbeing).
- Action opportunities: Making healthy choices, reflecting on routines, presenting to peers and families.
- Teacher reflection notes suggest more structured formative checkpoints and more student-led research in future iterations.

ci: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity.
lo: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating art

s.d: Students photograph their own artwork or a favourite classroom art piece, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the image and record a short voiceover explaining the inspiration, emotions, or story behind the piece. This brings visual art to life and lets students practice artistic reflection and digital storytelling in a playful, highly visual way.

Each student creates a digital 'Imagination Journal' using Book Creator, combining their own artwork, poetry, and voice recordings. They can add photos of their creative work, type or dictate short reflections, and use drawing tools to illustrate their ideas, celebrating diverse forms of expression and building a multimedia portfolio.

Students design digital posters or collages celebrating different forms of artistic expression (art, music, poetry, dance) using Canva's templates and drag-and-drop features. They can use the 'Remove Background' tool to insert themselves into famous artworks or creative scenes, fostering appreciation and playful engagement with art history.

Students use the iPad camera to document their creative process (e.g., painting, sculpting, performing), then upload photos or short videos to Seesaw. They add voice comments to explain their choices and feelings, creating a digital gallery that celebrates imagination and allows for peer and family feedback.

In small groups, students use Stop Motion Studio to create short stop-motion animations that tell a story or express an emotion using clay, paper, or classroom objects. This hands-on digital project encourages teamwork, fine motor skills, and creative storytelling through visual media.

Students will design and build imaginative 'story machines' that physically tell a simple story using movement and sound. Working in small groups, students use recycled materials, cardboard, craft supplies, and simple mechanisms (pulleys, levers, spinning parts) to create a device that visually represents a story scene or poem they have written in class. They will integrate a Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot as a moving 'character' that triggers different parts of their machine (e.g., rolling over a lever to make a paper flower bloom, activating a sound effect using bells or chimes). Students will use the Design Cycle: empathize (choose a story/scene), define (what actions and sounds will express it?), ideate (sketch and plan), prototype (build with materials and tech), test (run the machine and refine), and share (present to the class, explaining their design choices and creative process).

plannerText: The unit 'How We Express Ourselves' explores artistic appreciation and expression, focusing on how imagination and creativity are inspired through diverse forms such as art, poetry, and music. Students engage in hands-on art making, poetry writing, music exploration, and digital reflection, celebrating their own creativity and learning to appreciate the creative works of others.
humanVerifiedResetReason: Suggestion edited, swapped, regenerated or changed after human verification
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity

LINES OF INQUIRY: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating other's creativity and imagination

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Perspective, Communication

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Bus Stop Exploration in two classrooms. In Jane's room: students explored books from the library collection and artefacts such as art pieces, photos, and instruments. In Alex's room: students discussed what creativity means to them and watched ClickView short clips linked to artistic expression. Students completed a "Unit of Inquiry Detective" template and a See, Think, Wonder sheet (though this was found to be challenging for Year 2 learners). There was also an "I am poem" activity, where students typed their poem in Book Creator and published an artwork with watercolour paint.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Poetry writing in pairs
- Learner Profile Caring Detective activities
- Essential Agreement reflections
- "Size of the problem" and "Zones of Regulation" wellbeing activities
- "Beautiful Oops" exploration
- Exploring Art and Music forms as 'data collectors' using frayer models, tables, and labelled diagrams
- Listening to different musical instruments and discussing subject-specific vocabulary
- Creating pet portraits combining knowledge of various art styles and skills learnt in Art lessons with Popi Roumeliotous
- Producing several pieces of Art and exploring composition through Music apps such as Chrome Music Lab and Blob Orchestra
- Be Balanced choice boards (guided meditations, drawing tutorials, reading about the Arts, listening to music, watching dance performances)
- Haiku chalk poems in the Music Courtyard
- Year 2 class concerts

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing production of Art pieces
- Exploration of composition through Music apps
- Haiku chalk poems published in the Music Courtyard
- Year 2 class concerts
- Home learning project provides choice and agency
- Students co-constructed learning goals and success criteria, co-created word walls, and were involved in deciding how to display artworks and share learning on Seesaw

TEACHER NOTES: 
- The unit was highly transdisciplinary, with connections to Art, Music, Maths, and Writing.
- Students wrote poems in response to artworks and music, explored 2D and 3D shapes through art, and applied knowledge from Specialist classes.
- Students brought in materials and artefacts from home, including books connected to the Learner Profile and artworks made outside of school.
- The central idea was found to be hard for students to access and could benefit from simplification.
- Students found thinking routines challenging and needed teacher modelling and exposure.
- Students need support with organisational thinking skills and how to collect/record data; sketch noting was introduced as an option.
- Opportunities for greater collaboration with Specialist teachers are recommended for future iterations.
- Technology integration included Book Creator for poetry/art publishing, Chrome Music Lab, Blob Orchestra, and Seesaw for sharing learning.
- Students were highly engaged, especially with hands-on activities and sharing their work with the community.

ci: Marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities.
lo: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

s.d: Students photograph their handmade marketplace products or prototypes, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the objects and 'pitch' their product, explaining how it meets a specific need or want in the community. This brings empathy and persuasive communication into the design thinking process in a playful, visual way.

Learners design persuasive posters for their marketplace products using Canva's highly visual templates and 'Remove Background' feature. They can add photos of their prototypes, icons, and speech bubbles to clearly communicate the difference between needs and wants.

Students create a digital class book documenting the design thinking journey: identifying community needs, brainstorming, prototyping, and reflecting on feedback. Each page can feature photos, voice recordings, and drawings, making the process accessible and engaging for Year 2.

Students record short video reflections or photo journals as they test and improve their product designs. Teachers and peers can leave feedback using Seesaw's commenting tools, supporting the 'Reflective' learner profile and iterative improvement.

Students use the iPad Camera to document their product prototypes and the steps of the design process. These images can be used in digital portfolios, posters, or presentations, supporting visual storytelling and evidence of learning.

Students identify a real need in their community (e.g., helping people carry groceries, keeping pets safe, or improving playground safety). Using the design cycle, they brainstorm and sketch a solution, then build a moving automaton (mechanical model) that demonstrates how their idea works. Students use cardboard, popsticks, recycled materials, and simple cams/levers to create the physical structure. They integrate a Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot by designing the automaton so the robot can trigger movement (e.g., driving onto a pressure plate, activating a lever, or spinning a cam). The final showcase features each automata in action, with students explaining how their invention addresses a community need and how they iterated their design based on peer feedback.

plannerText: This unit explores how marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities, with a strong focus on the design thinking process. Students identify and empathise with real needs in their community, brainstorm and prototype solutions, and iterate based on feedback. Activities include needs vs wants games, home connections, vocabulary building, persuasive poster creation, and a culminating project where students design and showcase a product that addresses a community need.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Marketplaces reflects the needs and wants of communities. *Please note that this unit will be more focused on needs, wants using the 'design thinking' process to meet the needs of the community rather than selling items in marketplaces in 2025.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection; Responsibility; Function

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
- Stranded On An Island Provocation
- HWOO- Wants vs Needs Provocation (Year 2.pptx)
- Classroom Needs and Wants Poll: Students vote on whether items (e.g., food, computer games, water, toys) are a "Need" or a "Want" and discuss their choices.
- Learners bring two objects from home that they believe are a want or a need; use Think, Feel, Care thinking routine; discuss poverty and where it is seen in the community.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Needs vs. Wants Sorting Game: Using pictures or flashcards, students sort items into "Need" or "Want" piles and discuss.
- Home learning: Visit a market, take photos of 4 items you may need and want.
- Build a Vocabulary Wall Together: Develop shared definitions and examples for needs, wants, basic needs, and luxury wants.
- "I Want, I Need" Song or Chant: Create and sing a chant about needs and wants.
- Needs and Wants Role-Play: In pairs or groups, students role-play scenarios (e.g., camping trip) and separate items into needs and wants.
- Healthy Eating (Needs for Health): Sort foods into "Healthy" and "Unhealthy" categories and create a poster.
- Need or Want Collage: Cut out magazine pictures and sort into needs and wants on a poster.
- Venn diagram and story retell: Compare lives of different people/characters using mentor texts (Safe Same But Different, Those Shoes, Maddi's Fridge, Beatrice's Goat).
- Design Thinking Process: Prototyping, Tinker Time in the Maker Space, possible visit to a Design Class.
- Students design a product that responds to the needs of the immediate community (e.g., peers, younger students, elderly relatives).
- Excursion: Walk to Sacred Heart Mission in Glen Huntly Road to explore its purpose and take action.

ASSESSMENT:
- Evidence of learning includes participation in discussions, sorting activities, collages, role-plays, and the design and prototyping of a product to meet community needs.
- Sharing learning at a Share Fair showcasing student innovations/products.

TEACHER NOTES:
- Opportunity to connect to the SDG Zero Poverty.
- Strong focus on the design thinking process: empathising, creating, testing, and improving products to meet community needs.
- Local connection with Sacred Heart Mission and action opportunities.
- Maths integration: Addition and subtraction number stories.
- English integration: Retelling stories, using Venn diagrams, persuasive posters.
- Digital skills: Any teacher can connect digital tools to the unit.
- Tinker Time and Maker Space are key practical components.
- Possibility to connect to the text The Lost Thing by Shaun Tan.
- Transdisciplinary connections are encouraged but some specialist areas (Music, Visual Art, PE, Dance & Drama, Languages) are to be completed by respective teachers.
- Action opportunities include participation, advocacy, and social justice.
- Integration notes: Use of Seesaw for sharing, vocabulary wall, and collaborative activities.

ci: Forces and motion are all around us.
lo: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

s.d: Students use the iPad camera to capture sequences of objects in motion (e.g., toy cars, balls, or playground equipment) and create stop-motion animations in Stop Motion Studio. They can visually demonstrate different types of forces (push, pull, gravity, friction) and narrate what is happening in each frame, making the invisible visible in a highly creative way.

Students design digital posters or infographics illustrating different forces and simple machines found in their school playground. Using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature, they can insert themselves into action photos, label forces at play, and create vibrant, visual explanations of motion.

Students document their experiments and discoveries about forces and motion by creating interactive digital books. They can add photos, voice recordings, and drawings to explain concepts like friction, gravity, and simple machines, making their learning journey accessible and engaging for all.

Students record short video reflections or demonstrations of force experiments (e.g., rolling objects down ramps, testing friction on different surfaces) and share their thinking with peers and families. Teachers can prompt students to use key vocabulary and label forces in their videos.

Students watch curated short videos about forces, motion, and simple machines, then respond with their own video explanations or digital drawings showing what they learned. Teachers can use ClickView's interactive features to pause and annotate videos, prompting students to predict outcomes or identify forces in action.

s.t: Forces Fairground: Build & Code a Mini Amusement Ride
s.d: Students use the Design Cycle to invent and construct their own mini amusement park ride (e.g., spinning swings, Ferris wheel, or seesaw) using recycled materials, cardboard, popsticks, and connectors. They integrate a simple programmable robotics kit (like a Sphero Indi or a micro:bit with a motor) to power or animate part of their ride. For example, students build a cardboard Ferris wheel and use the micro:bit's motor to rotate it, or design a spinning ride that Sphero Indi can trigger. Along the way, students test and refine their builds to explore how pushes, pulls, and forces make their rides move, documenting each step with labeled diagrams and observations. The project culminates in a 'Forces Fairground' showcase where students demonstrate their ride in action and explain the forces at work.

plannerText: The Year 2 'How the World Works' unit explores forces and motion through hands-on experiments, playground investigations, and the study of simple machines. Students engage in activities such as classifying pushes and pulls, experimenting with ramps and friction, exploring magnetism and gravity, and culminating in building Rube Goldberg machines to demonstrate their understanding of forces in action.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Forces and motion are all around us

LINES OF INQUIRY: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Function, Connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Year 2 will go on an excursion to Twisted Science for a provocation to the HTWW unit. In the Maker Space, students will immerse themselves with wooden simple machines, cars and ramps, and building with Lego/screw sets. They will also use illustrations from "Ada Twist Scientist" for a graffiti wall brainstorm of key words connected to being a scientist.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Excursion to Twisted Science for hands-on experiments about forces.
- Maker Space immersion with wooden simple machines, cars and ramps, Lego/screw sets.
- Forces workshops and literacy connection through a Forces Poem.
- Tinkering with simple machines and brainstorming main features using 3D models.
- Push and Pull: Tuning in video, walk around the school to classify objects as push or pull, take photos and label.
- Magnetism Workshop: Testing items for magnetism, floating magnet experiment, magnet maze creation.
- Gravity: Dripping paint art with different viscosities, object drop using slow-mo camera on iPad.
- Friction: Playground investigation, friction experiments with book/lego ramp and toy car, testing wheel and axle with weights and skateboards.
- Wedge and inclined plane: Experiments with ramps and play dough, classifying wedges.
- Screw and lever: Linked Google presentations.
- Non-fiction text features: Use mentor texts, Seesaw task, writing information reports on simple machines.
- Forces Fact Collecting: Research skills, data chart, videos, Get Epic books, body actions for friction, gravity, push, pull.
- Rube Goldberg Machines: Students construct machines as a summative task, demonstrating understanding of simple machines and forces.
- Reflection on "Ada Twist Scientist" and connection to the Learner Profile.
- Sharing learning at student-led conferences, modeling experiments and sharing journals.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Students experiment with machines (push, pull, friction, magnet, gravity) and analyze results.
- Evidence: Students use results to make conclusions about different forces.
- Rube Goldberg Machine building as a summative task, demonstrating understanding through planning, design, labeling forces, and constructing with a partner.
- Sharing journals and modeling experiments at student-led conferences.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Maths (measurement, time, data collection), English (non-fiction text features, information reports, vocabulary building), Science (forces, energy, invention, materials), PSPE (movement, balance, coordination, physical forces), and Music (digital composition project with First Nations perspectives).
- Opportunities for digital skills: Use of iPads for slow-mo video, Seesaw for tasks, Book Creator for publishing, and digital sound resources in Music.
- Action opportunities: Students bring materials from home, model experiments for families, and connect learning to local playgrounds and community.
- Transdisciplinary connections are emphasized, with explicit links to PE, Music, and Library.
- Recommendations for future: Incorporate more visual tools (diagrams, slow-motion video) and scaffold digital sound use in Music.
- Highly engaging unit with strong student agency, hands-on learning, and language development.

ci: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make.
lo: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our planet

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app to interact with 3D models of Earth's resources, pollution, and conservation efforts. They can explore virtual composting, recycling, and water cycles, then create their own digital dioramas to demonstrate how everyday choices impact the planet. This hands-on AR experience makes abstract environmental concepts tangible and memorable for Year 2 learners.

Students design persuasive posters or infographics using Canva's visual templates and 'Remove Background' feature. They create campaigns encouraging their peers and families to adopt sustainable habits, such as reducing lunchbox waste or saving water, directly linking to the unit's focus on choices and environmental care.

Learners create a collaborative digital book documenting their week-long lunchbox waste audit. Each student adds a page with photos (taken using the iPad Camera), drawings, and voice recordings explaining how their choices reduced waste, reinforcing the connection between personal action and environmental impact.

The class builds a shared Padlet wall to collect and categorize examples of Earth-friendly actions at home and school. Students post photos, drawings, or short videos, then comment on each other's contributions, fostering a sense of community responsibility and idea-sharing around caring for the planet.

Teachers curate a playlist of short, age-appropriate videos on topics like recycling, composting, and water conservation. Students watch and then complete a visual reflection task, such as drawing or sequencing images, to show how the choices featured in the videos help care for Earth.

Students use the Design Cycle to invent and build model animal shelters that protect local wildlife and demonstrate sustainable living. First, they research native animals and the environmental challenges they face. In teams, students design and physically construct shelters using recycled materials, cardboard, fabric scraps, and natural items. They then integrate simple tech by embedding a Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot inside their shelter as an 'animal' to test accessibility, safety, and weather protection. Using iPads, students document their process, capture time-lapse videos of building, and record audio reflections on design choices. The project culminates in a 'Habitat Expo' where each group demonstrates their shelter's features and explains how their design helps both animals and the planet.

plannerText: The unit 'Sharing the Planet' explores how taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make. Students investigate Earth's resources, how actions affect the environment, and practical ways to care for the planet, including waste audits, persuasive campaigns, and hands-on action projects like no-waste lunches and community clean-ups.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make

LINES OF INQUIRY: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our Earth

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Causation, Change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
CEREs- Towards Zero Waste: Students will go on an excursion to CERES (October 14) as a provocation to the unit. Additional provocations include a picture story book provocation and a lunchbox waste collection across a week, sorting food waste for composting, recyclables, and general waste. Students will also listen to the Jack Johnson "3Rs" song.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Excursion to CERES focused on waste and sustainability.
- Picture story book provocation.
- Lunchbox waste audit and sorting (compost, recycle, general waste).
- Investigation of Earth's resources (plants, animals, water, trees, rocks, soil, sunlight, wind, air, human affection).
- Waste management learning (focus on 5Rs: reduce, reuse, recycle, rot, refuse).
- Air and water pollution investigations.
- Clean energy exploration.
- Use of Junior Landcare Learning Centre resources.
- Personalised inquiry project: students take action to care for the environment.
- Writing a persuasive speech to convince school leaders to make a positive change in the school environment.
- Walking excursion to care for the environment.
- Nude food celebration day and no waste lunch (food tech experience).
- Creation of a waste timeline showing how long different objects take to break down.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Sharing persuasive team speeches with the parent community.
- Personalised inquiry project presentations.
- Creation and sharing of a waste timeline.
- Evidence of learning through participation in excursions, speeches, and action projects.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- PSPE: While not directly connected, transdisciplinary thinking is supported during the Athletics unit by focusing on Responsibility and Connection. Students reflect on how their actions demonstrate personal responsibility and connection to others during group events.
- Opportunities for digital integration include documenting waste audits, persuasive speeches, and inquiry projects using digital tools (not specified which).
- Action opportunities are embedded through excursions, inquiry projects, and community sharing.
- Transdisciplinary connections are possible with Science (resources, pollution), English (persuasive writing), and PSPE (responsibility, fairness).
- Integration with Wellbeing curriculum is possible but not specified.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history.
lo: The significance of places to different people and cultures; The connection between landmarks and identity; How landmarks and symbols represent our history

s.d: Students use Podcast Equipment to conduct and record short interviews with family members or community elders about local landmarks and their stories. They produce a podcast episode that captures personal memories, historical facts, and cultural significance, bringing to life the voices connected to places around Elsternwick. This audio collection helps preserve and share diverse perspectives on how landmarks tell stories about history.

Students take photos of local landmarks during a campus or neighbourhood walk using the iPad Camera app, then upload them to Seesaw. They create interactive story maps by adding voice recordings and digital drawings to explain why each place is significant and what stories it tells, making connections between place and history.

Students use PicCollage to create a vibrant collage of images, drawings, and maps representing a landmark's features and surroundings. They then use ChatterPix Kids to animate a photo of the landmark, recording a short story or fun fact from the landmark's 'perspective' to bring its history to life.

Students research a local Indigenous landmark and, using Book Creator, craft a digital picture book retelling its Dreaming story or origin. They combine their own illustrations, audio narration, and simple text, directly linking to the exploration of how places are remembered and stories are preserved.

Students watch a Clickview documentary segment about a famous Victorian landmark, then use Field Guide to Victoria to investigate its flora, fauna, and cultural stories. They create a short video or illustrated fact sheet summarising what makes the place unique and why it is important to different groups.

s.t: Time Capsule Landmarks: Interactive Story Trail
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build 3D models of significant local or global landmarks using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and craft supplies. Each team embeds a simple programmable device (e.g., Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot) into their landmark's display, creating an interactive 'story trail.' Students program the robot to travel between landmarks along a custom-built map or pathway, pausing at each stop to trigger a physical 'story station'?such as a pop-up fact card, a QR code linking to a student-recorded oral history, or a hand-crafted diorama scene that reveals part of the landmark's story. The project follows the Design Cycle: students research, sketch, prototype, test robot navigation and story triggers, and refine their models.
