Students follow the Design Cycle: empathising by interviewing classmates about wellbeing needs, defining a focus (e.g., tracking hydration, reminding for movement breaks, mood check-ins), ideating designs, prototyping with both tech and craft, testing with peers, and improving their wearable. The project culminates in a 'Wellbeing Catwalk,' where students demonstrate their creations and explain how their choices support health and wellbeing.

plannerText: This unit explores how the choices and actions students make contribute to their health and wellbeing. Activities include investigating nutrition, exercise, sleep, and mindfulness, engaging with guest speakers, analysing data, and creating personal health action plans. Students reflect on their learning and take action to improve their own wellbeing.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The choices and actions we make contribute to our health and wellbeing.

LINES OF INQUIRY: What it means to be healthy; Choices and their impact on our health; Actions we can take to maintain a healthy mind and body

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, Causation, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: See, Think, Wonder ? using 10 different images linked to health & wellbeing. Students discuss connections between images and any big questions. Students complete a thinking routine 'colour symbol image' to explore the essence of health. Unpacking the central idea ? students are given a jumbled up central idea and work in small groups to put it together, then unpack and discuss each word. Creation of a word wall of vocabulary related to the inquiry. Students discuss what being healthy means, how they feel when healthy or not, and brainstorm ways to keep healthy.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Mindfulness/brain breaks (Cosmic Kids Yoga, Smiling Mind, meditation, body scan)
- Investigate bones and muscles; learn about food, exercise, and sleep impacts on health
- Explore nutrition, food groups, and benefits of different foods (using videos, Australian Guide to Healthy Eating diagram, books like 'Eat Well')
- Guest speakers: Dietician (balanced diet), Doctor (digestive system), Head of Student Wellbeing (sleep, emotions)
- Healthy Habits video and scenarios; students reflect on learning from guest speakers and set personal health goals
- Sleep and exercise lessons (videos, handouts, exercise tracker, healthy lunchbox creation)
- Maths: Data investigation for healthy eating and sleep, tally and graphing, interpreting health data
- Science: Generation Genius video on energy transfer from food
- UN Global Goals Zero Hunger ? Eat Better; reflect on lunchbox choices
- Note-taking skills, vocabulary building, and use of non-fiction texts
- Students create posters, videos, or comics to share knowledge
- Students create a healthy lunchbox using provided ingredients and track exercise for a week

ASSESSMENT: 
- Written notes in See, Think, Wonder template; oral and written responses
- Cloze activities, diagrams, and facts sharing
- Reflection on guest speakers (3 things learnt, 2 wonderings, 1 question)
- Summative assessment: student health advice profiles (PMI chart on what a child is doing well, not well, and what could be improved)
- Observed sharing of knowledge with the community (posters, digital, etc.)
- Homework tasks and lunchbox reflections
- Peer feedback through class sharing and discussion

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Consider linking body systems to digital systems in the next UoI for better transdisciplinary connections
- The unit is intense to start the year; guest speakers need an hour including Q&A
- A visit from a dentist would expand hygiene learning
- Mental health could be explored more if wellbeing staff are unavailable
- More mindfulness/brain breaks could be included
- Consider a Health & Wellbeing Expo as a future summative task
- UoI word bank cut and paste matching activity is particularly good for EAL/D students
- Students enjoyed guest speakers and real-life discussions; growth was evident in lifestyle changes
- Some students found it easier to identify positives and negatives than to recommend changes in the assessment profiles
- Technology suggestions: Use Seesaw for sharing and assessment, digital posters or comics, videos, and data collection/graphing software for maths integration

ci: Responding to different artistic expressions can promote thinking.
lo: Ways forms of expression convey messages; Creative and critical thinking skills; The language used to respond to The Arts

s.d: Students create immersive 3D art installations in Delightex that express ideas inspired by their poetry, music, or visual artworks, applying creative and critical thinking skills from the unit. They then publish their interactive installations on a Padlet board, where peers explore and leave reflective comments using the language of artistic response, deepening their understanding of how different forms of expression convey messages. This collaborative digital gallery connects directly to the unit?s central idea by promoting thoughtful engagement with diverse artistic expressions and preparing students for their student-led art show.

Students create digital artist statements and visual reflections using Canva, combining images of their own artwork, poetry, and excursion photos with layered text and design elements to communicate the mood, message, and inspiration behind their creative pieces.

Students compose and record their own audio reflections responding to diverse artistic expressions encountered during the NGV and State Theatre excursions. They script and produce short podcasts that explore how different art forms convey messages and stimulate creative and critical thinking, sharing their insights through engaging storytelling on Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com). These podcasts become a dynamic part of the student-led art show, amplifying the unit?s central idea through voice and sound.

Students create vibrant digital paintings inspired by Yayoi Kusama?s signature patterns and colours, experimenting with layering and brush effects to express emotions linked to poetry and music studied in the unit. They then share their artworks in a virtual gallery walk, reflecting on how different artistic choices influence viewers? thinking and feelings.

Students use Micro:bit to compose and perform digital music that expresses their responses to different artworks, blending technology and the arts. They experiment with sound, rhythm, and code to communicate emotions and ideas, deepening their creative and critical thinking. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=musical-microbit-unit-overview)

Students use the Design Cycle to explore how emotions can be expressed through movement and visual art. In teams, they brainstorm and sketch ideas for a kinetic sculpture that visually represents a chosen emotion (e.g., joy, curiosity, surprise). Using recycled materials, cardboard, craft sticks, and art supplies, they build a physical sculpture featuring moving parts powered by a simple motor or a Sphero Indi robot hidden inside the base. Students program the Sphero Indi or wire up the motor to create specific movement patterns that reflect their emotion (e.g., spinning for excitement, gentle swaying for calm). They decorate the sculpture in the style of Yayoi Kusama, integrating bold colours and patterns. Finally, students present their kinetic art at the student-led art show, explaining their design choices and how the movement and visual elements express their selected emotion.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in diverse artistic expressions, including poetry, visual art inspired by Yayoi Kusama, music composition, and excursions to the NGV and State Theatre. Students analyse, create, and respond to art forms, culminating in a student-led art show where they display and reflect on their own creative works.

The final gallery lets visitors interact with each portrait, experiencing the creator's unique artistic and emotional expression through both physical art and digital response.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Responding to different artistic expressions can promote thinking.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The ways in which forms of expression convey messages; Creative and critical thinking skills; The language used to respond to The Arts

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Perspective, Interpretation

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Provocation: Visit the Acquisitive Art Prize 2025 in Coates Pavilion to get students thinking about art?what did we see? What types of art did we see? How did it make you feel? What did you think about it?

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Unpack the Central Idea?students brainstorm key concepts and develop a Central Idea from these concepts.
- Poetry: Completed during Reading and Writing activities (Week 6-8); students observed on whether they could stick to the rules around different styles of poetry; students published their favourite poems to be displayed during the art show.
- Poetry analysis: Analysing figurative language during reading and writing sessions.
- Reading: Analysing a painting/picture?salience and composition.
- Writing: Different types of poems?haiku, diamante, shape, limerick, ballad.
- Excursion to NGV and State Theatre: How to analyse art.
- Analysing song lyrics (Bruno Mars)?how is this similar to poetry.
- Responding to photos of art from the excursions; providing opinions and making connections between works.
- Instrumental Music Program (Week 7-9): Students choose an art form to explore and create their own piece to display in the Year 4 Art Show; students plan the purpose, mood, message, and media type for their piece; create a plaque for their art piece.
- Use of PowerPoint to explain and support the art piece planning process.
- Seesaw Task for reflection and sharing.

ASSESSMENT: Students' favourite poems published and displayed during the art show; teacher observations and feedback on poetry and art analysis; peer feedback on poems; self-assessment and peer assessment for art show pieces; Seesaw Task for reflection.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Transdisciplinary connections include Visual Art (inspired by Yayoi Kusama?exploring repetition, bold colours, geometric shapes, and dynamic compositions) and Music (creating compositions inspired by images, using music language to describe connections).
- Library: Introduction of "Book Chat" to encourage wide reading and discussion, with students expressing opinions and reflecting on books.
- Early Term 3: Musica Viva incursion.
- Suggestion to book an MSO visit on the same day as NGV and State Theatre in future years.
- Note that Inquiry books from the Library were not used as much?could use more books with pictures of famous and indigenous paintings, and more about poems.
- Student agency: Students requested their own art show after enjoying the Acquisitive Art Prize; students chose their favourite art piece to analyse and discuss.
- Technology integration: Use of PowerPoint for planning, Seesaw for reflection and sharing, and digital publishing of poems and art show plaques.
- Reflection: Consider more time for art show preparation, possible integration of assessment tasks across specialist areas, and allowing students to create pieces in specialist lessons.

ci: Communities create rules, laws and services to help people live together fairly and safely.
lo: Effective and influential leadership; Strengths that help leaders guide others; How leadership impacts communities

s.d: Students will use Minecraft Education's 'Revamp Melbourne' lesson to collaboratively redesign a part of Melbourne, focusing on how rules, laws, and services shape fair and safe communities. They will justify their design choices and reflect on how their virtual city meets the needs of its citizens, directly linking to the central idea of community organisation.

Students create a digital 'Community Noticeboard' using Padlet, posting examples of local rules, laws, and services they research or observe in their neighbourhoods. They can comment on each other's posts, discuss fairness and safety, and propose improvements, fostering real-world connections to the unit.

Students script and record short podcasts interviewing 'community leaders' (role-played by peers or actual guests) about the importance of rules and services. They edit and publish episodes, building communication skills and deepening understanding of how leadership and laws impact daily life.

Students design and distribute a digital survey using Microsoft Forms to gather data from peers or family about which community services are most valued and why. They analyse the results in class, discussing how community needs drive the creation of rules and services.

Students use Canva to design infographics or posters explaining a specific community rule, law, or service, highlighting its purpose and impact. They can use the 'Remove Background' feature to add photos of their local area, making the project visually engaging and relevant.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large-scale, interactive board game that simulates how communities create and enforce rules, laws, and services. Using cardboard, recycled materials, markers, and clay, students physically build the game board, player pieces, and 3D structures representing community spaces (e.g., parks, schools, council buildings). They use iPads or Sphero Indi robots as 'community leaders' or 'decision-makers' that move across the board, triggered by digital QR codes or NFC tags embedded in the physical board. These codes activate student-created digital scenarios (e.g., short videos, audio dilemmas) that require players to make decisions about rules, leadership, or services. Students follow the Design Cycle: Investigate community needs, Plan the board layout and game mechanics, Create physical and digital components, Test the game with peers, and Reflect on how their rules and leadership decisions impact fairness and safety in their simulated community.

plannerText: This unit explores how communities create rules, laws, and services to help people live together fairly and safely. Students investigate effective leadership, the function of organisations, and the impact of societal decision-making through scenarios, guest speakers, team-building activities, and persuasive speeches for leadership roles.

They design routes that reflect how rules and services help keep people safe, collaborating to demonstrate leadership and problem-solving in real-world scenarios. This hands-on activity brings the unit?s focus on community organisation and safety to life through engaging robotics.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students work in teams to design persuasive digital posters or infographics advocating for a new community service or law, using Canva's design tools and 'Remove Background' feature to create visually compelling arguments.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and build their own virtual community in Minecraft Education, making decisions about rules, laws, and services to ensure fairness and safety for all citizens.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/active-citizen"},{"t":"Justice in Action: Community Courtroom Makerspace","d":"Students work in teams to design, build, and role-play a miniature courtroom and town square using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Teams use iPads to document their process and record mock 'court cases' where they debate and enact fair solutions, integrating digital storytelling with hands-on construction and collaborative problem-solving.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Leadership demonstrates people's strengths to reflect and take action.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Effective and influential leadership; Strengths that leaders demonstrate; Actions that leaders take

KEY CONCEPTS: Form & Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Pre-test?students create a mind map about leadership, including what leaders look like, sound like, and feel like. Students record what they think and wonder about leadership on sticky notes and have a class discussion about the responses. Provocation: Watch a Kid President video about being a leader and discuss his strengths and weaknesses.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Students rank leadership qualities in order of importance and record their responses as photographs.
- Students are given playground/classroom scenarios where they must show what they would do as a leader to solve the problem.
- Watch the movie "A Bug's Life" and complete a template noticing which characters show leadership qualities and giving examples.
- Read leadership job descriptions, sort through and decide which positions suit them, and reflect on whether their strengths match the job description.
- Year 4 GW Leadership Day with guest speaker Imogen Kane (Youth Ambassador for UN) via Teams, including team-building activities on the basketball court.
- Cross Campus Leadership Day with team-building and Q&A sessions with Middle School and Senior School leaders.
- Students complete a leadership ranking activity again to compare their initial understanding with what they now know.
- Students write and deliver persuasive speeches for leadership roles.

ASSESSMENT: Student persuasive speeches for leadership roles; Seesaw Task?student self-reflection and teacher feedback rubric; Peer feedback?students listen to each other practice their speeches and vote on final speeches; Self-assessment rubric on Seesaw.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Students were supported to explore their personal strengths and weaknesses and leadership qualities they would like to work on. They had the choice to choose the types of leaders that matched their interests and strengths.
- Peer feedback and self-assessment were embedded.
- Transdisciplinary connections include Visual Art (studying leaders in art such as Yayoi Kusama and Emily Kame Kngwarreye, then creating their own artwork and written piece) and Music (discussing the role of the conductor as leader and how musicians can lead or communicate during a performance).
- Technology integration: Use of Seesaw for reflection, feedback, and assessment; use of PowerPoint for guest speaker presentations and student projects; photographs to document ranking activities.
- Teacher reflection notes suggest scaling back the size of leadership roles for Year 4 students and possibly renaming them as "ambassadors" to reduce pressure. All resources and information are now stored in a single folder for easier access in future years.

ci: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.
lo: How material properties determine their use; Material solutions for a sustainable future; How research solves problems in the natural world

s.d: Students begin by researching different material properties and their uses in sustainable design, documenting their findings and reflections in a multimedia Book Creator science journal. Next, they use Micro:bit's 'Volcano Animations' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=volcano-animations-unit-of-work) to code and animate a volcano eruption, visually demonstrating the impact of material choices in real-world scenarios (e.g., building materials for volcano-resistant structures). This App Smash allows students to combine rich digital storytelling with hands-on coding, deepening their understanding of how research and material science solve natural world problems.

s.t: Minecraft: Filtering Water On The ISS
s.d: Using the 'Filtering Water On The ISS' Minecraft Education lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/filtering-water-on-the-iss), students experiment with designing and testing water filtration systems, mirroring real-world engineering challenges. They explore how material properties (such as permeability and durability) determine the effectiveness of filtration, directly linking to the unit's focus on sustainable solutions and the impact of design on the environment. Students can iterate on their designs and reflect on how their choices affect both human and environmental wellbeing.

Students use Seesaw to document their research into sustainable materials and prototype development, capturing photos, videos, and reflections throughout the process. They then App Smash with Canva to design a persuasive digital poster or infographic pitching their sustainable product, highlighting the properties of chosen materials and their environmental impact. This workflow empowers students to synthesize research, design thinking, and communication skills, culminating in a polished, shareable artifact that advocates for sustainable innovation.

Students collaboratively build a digital gallery on Padlet, uploading images, videos, and short descriptions of various materials they investigate in class (e.g., metals, plastics, fibres, glass). Each entry includes observations about the material's properties, uses, and environmental impact, fostering peer-to-peer learning and critical analysis. This interactive resource becomes a living reference that supports inquiry into how material choices affect product design and sustainability.

Through the Micro:bit 'Electrical Conductors' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=electrical-conductors-unit-of-work), students design and conduct experiments to test which materials conduct electricity and which act as insulators. They record and analyze their data, connecting their findings to real-world applications such as designing safe, efficient, and sustainable products. This hands-on digital integration reinforces the unit's emphasis on investigating material properties and their practical implications.

s.t: Physical Makerspace: Animal Enrichment Prototype Challenge
s.d: In the Makerspace, students design and build a prototype of an animal enrichment toy or feeding machine using recycled and sustainable materials. They must consider the properties and environmental impact of each material, iterating on their designs based on testing and feedback. This hands-on challenge directly connects to the unit's focus on how material choices and design thinking can support animal wellbeing and promote sustainability in the natural world.

plannerText: Students investigate material properties, sustainability, and the impact of design on the natural world. Key activities include science experiments (e.g., sink/float, friction, magnetism), research and reflection in digital science journals, and the design and prototyping of animal enrichment devices using sustainable materials.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.

LINES OF INQUIRY: How material properties determine their use; What material solutions support a sustainable future; How research is used to solve problems in the natural world

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Function, Causation

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Challenge: Students build a structure using only natural materials found outside. Discuss the limitations and possibilities of these materials. Students write observations and reflections in their science journals on Book Creator. Additional provocations include a "Mystery Material" activity where students guess properties of unknown materials through observation and experimentation, and a sensory tunnel activity where students identify materials by touch while blindfolded.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Unpack the Central Idea in small groups by researching key words.
- Define and investigate different types of materials (fibres, metals, glass, plastic), their production, and decomposition.
- Create and maintain a science journal in Book Creator, recording observations, reflections, questions, and vocabulary.
- Maths integration: Calculate weight, volume, or strength of materials; explore measurement concepts.
- Reading and discussion about the purpose of pet toys and animal enrichment.
- Seesaw task: Learn about different materials and find classroom objects made from them.
- Addi Incursion: Observe enrichment toys for Addi (school animal), possibly visit sanctuary garden.
- Compare and contrast enrichment needs of domesticated pets and wild animals using Venn diagrams.
- Watch videos about animal enrichment and zoo practices.
- Conduct science experiments (sink and float, free falling, magnet, friction, mouldy bread) and record in journals.
- Compare historical and modern zoo practices.
- Design and create a prototype of a feeding machine or enrichment toy for an animal, considering sustainability and material impact.
- Test prototypes with classroom guinea pigs.

ASSESSMENT: Student self-reflection rubric; Teacher assessment rubric on Seesaw; Science journals in Book Creator; Prototypes of animal enrichment toys; Peer feedback; Observations and reflections.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Transdisciplinary connections with Maths (measurement, calculations) and Sustainability (material choices for prototypes).
- Integration with digital tools: Book Creator for science journals, Seesaw for tasks and assessment rubrics.
- Action opportunities: Students test their enrichment prototypes with real animals (guinea pigs).
- Recommendations for future years: More explicit modeling and structured research time, use of graphic organizers for animal research, and more modeling of enrichment's impact on animal wellbeing.
- Noted that students enjoyed hands-on activities and real-life application with guinea pigs, but needed more scaffolding to connect research to prototype design and animal wellbeing.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: Diversity of First Nations people; Importance of community, family and traditions; Importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app to create interactive 3D representations of significant First Nations sites, languages, or cultural artifacts. They can record audio explanations in local Indigenous languages or English, and classmates can explore these virtual objects by rotating the cubes, deepening understanding of Country and community.

Students write and illustrate their own Dreamtime-inspired stories, incorporating research about First Nations traditions, Country, and language. They can embed audio recordings of themselves reading in English and, where possible, in a local Indigenous language, then publish and share these digital books with younger students.

Using Canva, students design visual infographics or posters that highlight the diversity of First Nations communities, languages, and connections to Country. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to incorporate their own photos of artwork or excursions, and add QR codes linking to further resources or student-created videos.

Students collaboratively build a digital map or timeline on Padlet, pinning stories, artworks, and facts about different First Nations groups across Australia. Each post can include images, student reflections, and links to Dreamtime stories or interviews with elders, fostering a sense of shared inquiry.

Students use 'Minecraft: Revamp Melbourne' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/revamp-melbourne) to design a future Melbourne that respects and integrates First Nations perspectives on Country, language, and community. They must explain how their builds reflect Indigenous values and sustainable practices.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, winding 'story pathway' representing a Dreamtime journey across Country. Using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, natural elements, and paint, they build physical story stations along the path?each station depicts a key moment, animal, or landscape from a Dreamtime story. At each station, students embed a simple programmable device (e.g., Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot) with custom-printed scannable story codes or symbols. As the robot travels the path, it pauses at each station: students use iPads to scan the code, triggering digital content (audio, video, or AR overlays) they have created?such as retellings, soundscapes, or digital artwork?to enrich the physical model with layered storytelling and First Nations perspectives. The project follows the Design Cycle: empathise (explore Dreamtime stories and cultural meanings), define (decide on story moments to highlight), ideate (plan the pathway and interactive elements), prototype (build and test stations and robot integration), and evaluate (share with peers and reflect on connections to Country, language, and community).

plannerText: Students explore the significance of Country, language, and community to First Nations people through Dreamtime stories, art analysis, excursions (e.g., CERES, Yiramalay elders), and hands-on activities. They create narratives, artworks, and dioramas, engage with elders, and reflect on physical, cultural, and spiritual connections to Country.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The diversity of First Nations people; The importance of community, family and traditions to First Nations people; The importance of Country to First Nations people

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, Perspective, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Pre-test?students are given a template and asked to visualize their understanding of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander culture through drawing and writing. Provocation: Show students a range of indigenous symbols and artworks; students try to work out what the symbols represent and identify them in the artworks, discussing the meaning of the pieces. Students also meet with a group of Year 10-12 students and elders from the Yiramalay studio school, participate in a smoking ceremony with a local elder, play games, ask questions about culture and connection to country, and share lived experiences.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- CERES excursion: Introduction to country, bush tucker walk, recreating the story of Tiddalick the Frog, face and body painting with ochre and indigenous symbols, followed by a Seesaw reflection.
- Compare and contrast the Aboriginal map and a world map; watch a video about First Nations Spirituality and discuss connections to the "Who We Are" unit.
- Read and analyse Dreamtime stories (e.g., The Rainbow Serpent, The Cunning Crow), focusing on narrative structure and comparison to other narratives.
- Literacy sessions using "The Burnt Stick" to explore figurative language, author's purpose, and the impact of Europeans on First Nations people; discuss the Stolen Generation.
- Class novel: "Wylah the Koorie Warrior"?explores totems, guardians, Western settlers, and Indigenous Australian life.
- Students write and publish a Dreamtime-inspired story with a moral or lesson, using Book Creator, and read it to Prep buddies who provide feedback on Seesaw.
- Diorama project: Students create a diorama to demonstrate their understanding of Indigenous culture through spiritual, cultural, and physical lenses.
- Visual Arts: Create artwork inspired by First Nations Peoples artists, using printmaking techniques and exploring symbols and patterns; organize a small exhibition.

ASSESSMENT: Pre-test template (drawing/writing), Seesaw reflection tasks, published Dreamtime stories with Prep buddy feedback, diorama project demonstrating understanding of spiritual, cultural, and physical connections, self-assessment and peer feedback.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Visual Arts (printmaking, exhibition), Literacy (narrative writing, analysis), and camp experiences (Healesville Sanctuary, Indigenous elder visit).
- Use of Book Creator and Seesaw for publishing and reflection.
- Opportunities for action include engaging with local elders, creating child-friendly Acknowledgement to Country, and challenging stereotypes.
- Some time constraints due to school production; noted need for more time on sustainability and deeper synthesis at the end of the unit.
- Suggestion to move camp closer to this unit for better integration.
- Students enjoyed hands-on and creative activities (dioramas, excursions, Dreamtime stories).
- Technology suggestions: digital storytelling (Book Creator), digital reflections (Seesaw), collaborative word clouds (iPads), and possible virtual meetings with elders or remote communities.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities and new understandings.
lo: Reasons for exploration; How people have explored over time; Consequences of exploration

s.d: Students research a significant historical explorer or invention that changed the course of exploration. Using Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions, they design and build a 3D model of their chosen invention or a scene from a key exploration, then present their build to the class, explaining its historical impact and how it led to new understandings.

Students plot the journeys of famous explorers on Google Maps, annotating key stops with text, images, or links. They compare historical routes with modern geography, discussing how technology has changed our ability to explore and understand the world.

Students create a digital journal in Book Creator, documenting their own 'exploration' of a chosen topic, place, or historical period. They include multimedia elements such as photos, voice recordings, and maps to reflect on discoveries and new understandings.

Using Canva, students design an interactive timeline or infographic that illustrates the evolution of exploration methods and technologies over time, highlighting key discoveries and their consequences.

Students collaborate on a Padlet wall to share research about different explorers from various cultures and eras. Each student posts a summary, image, and a reflection on how that explorer's journey led to new opportunities or understandings.

s.t: Exploration Time Capsules: Past Meets Future
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a physical 'time capsule' inspired by a historical explorer's journey, using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and craft supplies. Each capsule must include hidden compartments and interactive elements. Teams embed a simple programmable device (e.g., a Sphero Indi or a Makey Makey circuit) that triggers lights, sounds, or movements when the capsule is opened or solved. The capsule's clues and artifacts represent discoveries, challenges, and cultural encounters from the explorer's era. Students use the Design Cycle to research, prototype, test, and refine both the physical build and the digital interactions, culminating in a hands-on museum where peers unlock and explore each other's time capsules to uncover the stories of exploration.

plannerText: This unit engages students in investigating the reasons for exploration, how people have explored across history, and the consequences of exploration. Activities include mapping journeys, researching explorers, writing historical narratives, and hands-on projects that connect exploration to discovery and new understandings.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities and new understandings.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Reasons for exploration; How people have explored over time; The consequences of exploration

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Pre-assessment using picture prompts?students view images about exploration (e.g., ship, telescope, microscope, sextant, compass, old and modern exploration) and write down their noticing, wondering, connections, and vocabulary. Images are placed in the communal space for a thinking routine: "What makes you say that?" Students brainstorm prior knowledge of 'discovery' and 'exploration'.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Unpacking the central idea through a word hunt: words from the central idea are hidden around the Junior School, and students work in teams to find and assemble them.
- Frayer model activities for 'exploration' and 'discovery' to clarify concepts.
- Exploration of the Middle School: students create a map of what they think the Middle School looks like, then adjust it after an actual visit.
- Investigate explorers who have led to discoveries, focusing on Australian explorers (e.g., Captain Cook, Burke and Wills, First Fleet) and then allowing students to choose their own explorer to research.
- Use of books and videos: "Meet Captain Cook," "I wish I'd sailed with Captain Cook," "Great Expedition," "Burke and Wills," "The First Fleet," and relevant YouTube documentaries.
- Inquisitive: Year 4 History, Lesson 1 (video and ebook), and "choose your own explorer" video.
- Literacy integration: students write historical narratives from a convict's point of view after researching convicts and explorers.
- Guest speakers: Andy Tomkins (Antarctica exploration, meteorite research, brought in a meteorite), Logan Roger's dad (Antarctica exploration).
- Home learning: students discuss their own explorations/discoveries and the opportunities/new understandings they found.
- Optional: students select an area of exploration to research and create a PowerPoint guided by the lines of inquiry.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Pre-assessment using picture prompts and Frayer models.
- Assessed integration of knowledge and facts into historical narratives.
- Summative assessment: students write historical narratives and/or present research using PowerPoint, PicCollage, Book Creator, posters, etc.
- Student self-assessment and peer feedback.
- Maps, data charts, wonderings, and children's work displayed in the classroom.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Technology integration: students use PowerPoint, PicCollage, Book Creator for presenting research; World Book and Britannica Online for research.
- Library sessions: focus on explorer stories and research skills, including referencing and distinguishing primary/secondary sources.
- Dance and Drama: role play to explore perspectives (e.g., Indigenous Australians vs. explorers/convicts).
- Maths integration: mapping skills.
- Some students found open-ended research challenging?more guidance recommended.
- Suggestion for more group and physical activities, and more explicit teaching on referencing and research skills.
- Guest speakers and excursions/incursions are highly valued; look for more opportunities.
- Transdisciplinary links with Literacy, Library, Dance and Drama, Maths, and Music (exploring music from different countries and time periods).

ci: People's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.
lo: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs; Contributions of different religions to human diversity

s.d: Students design and script interactive chatbots that simulate respectful interfaith dialogues. Using Wise Discussion Chatbots, students can program scenarios where users ask questions about different spiritual or religious beliefs, and the chatbot responds with accurate, inclusive information. This fosters empathy, digital citizenship, and a nuanced understanding of diversity in beliefs.

Students create visually engaging infographics or digital posters comparing symbols, places of worship, and practices from different world religions. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to highlight key religious artifacts or figures, supporting visual literacy and cross-cultural understanding.

Students author a digital 'Beliefs Around the World' book, compiling research, reflections, and interviews with family or community members about their beliefs. They can include audio recordings, images, and text, making the book accessible and multimodal.

Students curate a playlist of short documentary clips from Clickview that showcase rituals, festivals, and places of worship from various religions. They annotate each clip with their own reflections and questions, then share the playlist with peers for discussion.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'Belief Wall' on Padlet, posting images, facts, and personal stories about spiritual or religious diversity in their class and community. They can comment on each other's posts, fostering respectful dialogue and inquiry.

Students work in teams to research a world religion or spiritual tradition, focusing on a key festival or celebration. Using the Design Cycle, they plan and physically build a vibrant parade float representing that festival using recycled materials, cardboard, fabric scraps, and art supplies. Each float must include at least one moving or interactive element powered by simple robotics (e.g., a Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot hidden inside to drive the float, or a motorized feature using a simple circuit kit). Students program the bot to perform a symbolic 'parade route' through a custom-built track that winds through a collaborative classroom 'parade ground,' encountering physical landmarks or interactive stations representing values, symbols, or rituals from their chosen belief. The culminating showcase is a live parade where students introduce their floats, explain the meaning behind their design choices, and demonstrate the interactive features, highlighting how beliefs shape cultural celebrations and contribute to our community's diversity.

plannerText: This unit explores how people's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life, focusing on spiritual or religious beliefs, similarities and differences among religions, and the contributions of these beliefs to human diversity. Key activities include gallery walks of religious imagery, question walls for different religions, research and information reports on world religions, excursions to places of worship, and the creation of cultural posters and information reports.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: People's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs/religions; The contributions different religions/spiritual beliefs have on human diversity

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, perspective, connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Display a range of images from different religions/cultures from around the world. Students go on a gallery walk around the room and write on a yellow post-it note any thoughts or pink post-it note any questions they have about the image. During the unit, if students find an answer to a question, they can write the answer on a different coloured post-it note and add to the poster. Students think about it by themselves, then share with a partner, then with the class. Read and discuss the book "What do you believe?" and write an individual/class definition of what 'belief' means.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Read the picture book "All Kinds of Beliefs" and discuss what is important to them.
- Create question walls for each religion (All beliefs, Buddhism, Hinduism, Judaism, Christianity, Sikhism, Islam) using post-it notes for questions and answers.
- View Bunjil's Wing Kinetic Sculpture video and practice asking thin and thick questions.
- Visit the Chapel to explore different religions by smelling incense, lighting candles, and tasting bread and grape juice.
- Research Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Hinduism using mentor texts and ClickView videos, completing "My Religion Fact File" for each.
- Excursion to Uniting Church Glen Waverley to observe practices, ask questions, and reflect on Seesaw.
- Make connections to own beliefs and values and those of their family.
- Revisit all fact files and sort out different symbols, sacred texts, and places of worship.
- Watch Creation Stories video and complete a religions-sorting activity.
- Create cultural posters about themselves including home languages, favourite food, religion (if applicable), interests, and interesting facts.
- Students create an information report on one world religion, using sub-headings for different areas discussed.

ASSESSMENT: Information report on one world religion; Reflection task on Seesaw about the Uniting Church excursion; Student self-assessment and peer feedback

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Chaplain integration with this UOI is integral to its success.
- Excursions and VR experiences (virtual tour of a place of worship) are included.
- Bunjil art installation and Museum video tie in with Indigenous perspectives.
- Library and internet resources are used for research.
- Sustainable Development Goals links: Good Life Goals (Inspired Citizenship): 10- Be Fair (Reduced Inequalities), 11- Love Where You Live (Sustainable Cities and Communities), 17- Come Together (Partnerships for the Goals).
- Action opportunities include teaching others about their culture, completing service for a charity, and acts of kindness.
- Noted need for more explicit questioning about human diversity in pre-assessment.
- Integration with digital tools: Seesaw for reflection and assessment, use of ClickView for video resources, and Book Creator for information reports.

ci: People design for purpose.
lo: How design thinking creates in various ways; Design thinking meets community needs; Effective ways to engage an audience

s.d: Students use Minecraft: Education Edition to collaboratively design and build innovative solutions for Melbourne's future needs, directly connecting to the unit's focus on purposeful design and engaging with real-world community challenges. They must justify their design choices and reflect on how their creations meet current and future needs, mirroring the design thinking process.

Students script, record, and edit a podcast episode where they pitch their design solution for a chosen natural disaster scenario, using persuasive language and sound effects to engage an audience. This task supports the unit's emphasis on effective communication and pitching ideas.

Students create digital infographics or persuasive posters to visually communicate the impact of their design solution, integrating data, empathy maps, and stakeholder perspectives. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to enhance visuals without a green screen.

Students storyboard and animate the journey of their design from ideation to prototype, narrating the design thinking process and highlighting how their solution addresses community needs. This supports reflection and clear communication of process and purpose.

Students collaboratively document their research, empathy maps, and feedback cycles on a shared Padlet wall, enabling real-time peer feedback and iterative improvement of their design solutions. This creates a transparent record of the design thinking journey.

s.t: Rights in Action: Interactive Community Sculpture
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, interactive sculpture that represents a community responding to a natural disaster, highlighting UNICEF child rights in action. Using recycled materials (cardboard, fabric, plastic, wood scraps), students physically construct buildings, shelters, and key infrastructure. Embedded within their sculpture, teams integrate simple programmable electronics (e.g., Makey Makey, micro:bit, or littleBits) to add interactive features: for example, pressing a button lights up emergency shelters, triggers recorded messages about child rights, or activates moving parts (like a rescue drone or water pump). Students use iPads to plan, document, and code simple interactions. The final sculpture becomes a hands-on exhibit, inviting peers to explore how purposeful design can support communities and uphold rights during disasters.

plannerText: Year 5 students at Glen Waverley explore 'How We Express Ourselves' through the central idea 'People design for purpose.' The unit immerses students in the design thinking process as they investigate natural disasters, research community needs, and collaboratively prototype innovative solutions. Activities include mapping, data analysis, empathy mapping, ideation, prototyping, pitching, and reflection, with strong links to UNICEF's child rights and real-world problem solving.

This immersive, hands-on workflow makes abstract design concepts tangible and engaging.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students create persuasive digital pitch decks for their UNICEF-inspired design solutions, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to showcase their prototypes and integrate infographics, maps, and empathy maps. They craft movable barriers, construct safe zones, and create obstacles that mirror real-world challenges. Teams then program their Sphero Indi to autonomously navigate the custom-built terrain, deliver 'emergency supplies' (represented by small crafted tokens), and reach affected areas. Throughout, students document their design thinking process, test and iterate their physical and digital prototypes, and present their final rescue solutions to an audience, explaining how their design addresses both the hazard and community needs.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: People design for purpose

LINES OF INQUIRY: How the design thinking process helps to create in various ways (Function); Design thinking helps to meet the needs of different communities. (Causation) (Ethical and practical considerations); Effective ways to engage with an audience (Connection)

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Causation, Connection, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Workshops with UNICEF on Wed 25th June, including an introduction to the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child and a Mini Design Thinking Workshop. In Week 2, students engage with activities such as reading "Jackie French Fire," exploring nature's resilience, using a Geoscience mapping tool, and creating a class Wonder Wall with student questions. They also watch "Nature is Speaking," participate in "Step In, Step Out, Step Back," discuss provocation statements, vocabulary, and the Risk Triangle.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Jigsaw activity to deepen understanding of natural hazards.
- Bushfire focus: "See, Think, Wonder" on "The Summer Australia Burned," review of bushfire infographics, mapping bushfire risk using Geoscience Australia tools, and analysis of infrastructure impacts.
- Earthquake focus: National Geographic video, reading comprehension, Moment Magnitude Scale, class definition, tectonic plates jigsaw, and optional ClickView video.
- Flood focus: Compare before/after of Lismore, watch "Understanding Floods" and "Flooding Aftermath" videos, create Flood Survival Posters, and map exploration.
- Group work: Students choose a disaster topic and a child right, research locations, create topographical maps, analyze impacts, and develop empathy maps.
- Design thinking: Brainstorming (Crazy 8s), storyboarding, prototyping, refining solutions, and writing persuasive pitches.
- Pitching and presenting projects using UNICEF marking criteria.
- Visual Art: Creation of symbols for children's rights using gel plate printmaking and digital art on iPads.
- French: Presenting a play, exploring French poetry, and writing calligrammes.

ASSESSMENT: Prototype and pitch presentations (using UNICEF marking criteria), information reports, graphs representing data, Seesaw portfolio tasks, and reflection activities. Top teams selected for a semi-final.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong transdisciplinary connections with Visual Art (logo design, digital art), Library (natural disasters and empathy), Maths (data analysis, graphs), and French (performance, poetry).
- Technology integration includes iPads for digital art, mapping tools, and Seesaw for portfolio tasks.
- Emphasis on empathy, student agency, and collaborative project work.
- Recommendations for future: Consider shifting information report writing to a different unit to focus HWEO on projects; continue to scaffold empathy and use rubrics more effectively; review timeline for design thinking and front-load natural disaster content in a prior unit.
- Action opportunities include pitching real solutions and reflecting on their impact, with a focus on authentic connections and student voice/choice.

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

s.d: Students collaborate in Minecraft Education to redesign Melbourne for the future, focusing on how economic systems shape city infrastructure, trade, and community services. They must justify their design choices based on economic principles, simulate trade, and reflect on how their solutions meet both current and future needs of a diverse society.

Students create digital infographics or animated explainers that break down complex economic concepts such as taxation, budgeting, or the difference between needs and wants. These can be shared with the school community to promote financial literacy and spark discussion about economic responsibility.

Students use Excel to model personal and community budgets, track classroom 'income' and 'expenses' from simulated jobs or side hustles, and visualize data with graphs. This hands-on approach helps them understand financial decision-making and the impact of economic choices.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'idea wall' to share and debate what constitutes wealth, how income is managed, and the responsibilities of citizens in economic systems. They can post reflections, questions, and multimedia responses to real-world economic scenarios.

Students author interactive digital books that tell the story of a fictional character navigating different economic systems, making choices about earning, spending, and saving. They can embed audio reflections, videos, and images to bring economic concepts to life.

s.t: Market Movers: Interactive Trade Fair Booths
s.d: Students work in teams to design, build, and run their own mini-businesses by creating interactive trade fair booths. Using the Design Cycle, each team ideates a product or service, physically prototypes it with recycled materials, cardboard, popsticks, and craft supplies, and constructs a creative booth display. To integrate technology, students embed simple programmable circuits (e.g., using Makey Makey or micro:bit) into their booths to create interactive features?such as digital price displays, sound effects for transactions, or motion-activated advertisements. On Trade Fair Day, teams set up their booths in a simulated marketplace, use digital tablets to track transactions, and engage in live trading using a classroom currency system. This project deepens understanding of economic systems, entrepreneurship, and financial responsibility through hands-on creation, digital-physical integration, and authentic role-play.

plannerText: This unit explores how economic systems underpin human society, with students investigating concepts of wealth, income, and financial responsibility. Activities include provocations about what makes someone 'wealthy', debates on tax, simulated classroom economies, budgeting exercises, and the collaborative design of ideal communities within set financial constraints.

This fosters historical perspective and creative application.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions"},{"t":"Microsoft Excel","d":"Students use Excel to model personal or community budgets, experimenting with different income sources, expenses, and savings strategies. They create interactive spreadsheets that visually demonstrate the consequences of financial decisions, supporting critical thinking about economic responsibility and resource allocation.","url":""},{"t":"Wealth Web: Community Resource Exchange Game","d":"Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively invent and build a hands-on board game that models how communities organise, share, and grow different forms of wealth (not just money). In teams, they design a 3D 'community map' using cardboard, clay, and recycled materials to represent neighbourhoods, resources (like skills, time, food, knowledge), and opportunities. They then integrate programmable Sphero Indi robots as 'community couriers'?students code them to follow colour-coded tracks between locations, simulating the flow of resources and jobs. Game mechanics, designed and prototyped by students, include physical tokens, custom dice, and event cards, all crafted by hand. The final game is play-tested by peers, requiring players to cooperate, trade, and solve challenges to build a thriving, equitable community?making abstract economic concepts tangible and dynamic through creative making and digital-physical integration.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways (combine 2 & 3)

KEY CONCEPTS: Responsibility, function, perspective

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
Week 1 & 2
Lesson 1: Provocation - What is wealth? What makes you wealthy? Students respond to these questions to create an 'idea wall' and contribute ideas via a Chalk Talk. 
Lesson 2: The Treasure Box by Margaret Wild (audio and visual analysis, independent reading, response questions).
Lesson 1 (LOI 2): Provocation ? Rights and Responsibilities. Students sort Rights and Responsibilities Cards using a Venn Diagram, discuss as a class, and create a word wall. 
Lesson 2: Is tax good or bad? Class discussion and mini debate after watching "Village and the Boy Named Tax," followed by a Claim, Support, Question thinking routine.
Lesson 1 (LOI 3): Provocation ? Pocket Money. Class discussion about pocket money, earning, spending, and management.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Sorting Rights and Responsibilities Cards and Venn Diagram activity.
- Watching and responding to "The Treasure Box" and "Village and the Boy Named Tax."
- Banqer online modules: What is income? Budgeting for Success, Expenses & Savings, Interest on Savings, Careers & Employment (writing a CV, applying for jobs in classroom economy).
- Homework: Percentage Pizzas (family discussion about income and expenses, creating a pizza chart).
- Job interviews, creating business cards, pitching side hustles, creating advertisements.
- Needs vs Wants discussion and Seesaw portfolio task.
- Community values inquiry using the Australian Values statement and 3 Whys thinking routine.
- Teenage Boss episode viewing and reflection.
- Government spending inquiry using local maps, Budget Setting Activity (group work as ministries).
- Creating an ideal community: brainstorming, mapping with blocks, budgeting with a $1 million tax budget, drawing on isometric dot paper.
- Kindness in action: student-chosen action related to "Wealth is more than money."
- Visual Art: Pop Art project exploring mass production, consumerism, and art as investment.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Seesaw portfolio tasks (Needs vs Wants, Cause and Effect).
- Banqer activity completion.
- CV writing and job application in classroom economy.
- Budget Setting Activity group presentations.
- Ideal Community project (physical model, budget, isometric drawing).
- Student-chosen action project.
- Fable/poetry writing.
- Student self-assessment and peer feedback.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Integration with HASS, Maths, Language, and Visual Art (Pop Art, value of art, investment, artists like Banksy).
- Use of Banqer online platform for financial literacy.
- Encouragement of student agency: students manage their own money in Banqer, apply for jobs, choose side hustles, and select action projects.
- Opportunities for digital learning: Seesaw for portfolio tasks, Padlet for exit tickets, OneNote for responses, iPads for photographing community models and drawing.
- Emphasis on collaborative group work, critical and creative thinking, and real-world financial contexts.
- Action opportunities: kindness projects, community budgeting, and advocacy for local initiatives.
- Noted in teacher reflections: students manage their money in Banqer, apply for jobs, and choose their own action projects. Fable/poetry writing is included as a creative engagement.

If you need further breakdown by week or more detail on any section, let me know!

ci: The interactions between living things, materials, and light enable us to make decisions about the world
lo: How structural features and adaptations help living things survive; How weathering and erosion cause change to Earth's surface; How light is used in science and technology

s.d: Students will use Minecraft Education to explore the concepts of magnetism and gravity by designing a magnetic structure that could protect the International Space Station or assist its astronauts. This hands-on digital simulation directly connects to the unit's focus on the interactions between materials and forces, allowing students to experiment with invisible forces in a creative, collaborative virtual environment.

Students use Merge Cubes to create and explore interactive 3D models demonstrating how light interacts with different materials (transparent, translucent, opaque) and how these interactions affect living things. They can record their observations and share digital walkthroughs of their models, making abstract science concepts tangible and engaging.

Students collaboratively create interactive whiteboard presentations to model scenarios such as how light enables plants to grow (photosynthesis), or how materials are used in disaster-resistant building designs. They can annotate, animate, and narrate their explanations, supporting both science understanding and communication skills.

Students program Sphero BOLT robots to navigate obstacle courses that simulate natural hazards (e.g., bushfires, floods, earthquakes). They collect data on speed, distance, and light sensor readings, connecting robotics to real-world problem-solving and the unit's focus on decision-making in response to environmental challenges.

Students design persuasive infographics or digital posters using Canva to communicate the importance of making informed decisions about materials and light in disaster preparedness (e.g., bushfire-resistant materials, reflective surfaces for heat management). They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to enhance visuals with their own iPad photos.

s.t: Hazard Heroes: Smart Emergency Alert Landmarks
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build scale-model landmarks (e.g., bridges, towers, houses) from recycled cardboard, craft sticks, and other makerspace materials, embedding them with programmable micro:bit sensors or Makey Makey circuits. Each landmark must physically demonstrate how it detects and responds to a simulated natural hazard (bushfire, flood, or earthquake) using the sensors?e.g., flashing LEDs for fire, buzzer alarms for floods, or shaking platforms for earthquakes. Students code the micro:bits or circuits to trigger these responses based on real-world data (e.g., temperature, moisture, vibration), then physically prototype and decorate their landmarks to reflect local environments and communities. The project culminates with teams pitching their interactive models, explaining how their integrated tech and design choices help keep people and living things safe, and reflecting on empathy, scientific thinking, and iterative improvement throughout the Design Cycle.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 5 students in understanding natural hazards (bushfires, earthquakes, floods) and how the interactions between living things, materials, and light inform decision-making. Students engage in collaborative inquiry, data analysis, mapping, prototyping, and persuasive pitching, culminating in designing and presenting solutions to real-world challenges with a strong focus on empathy, scientific thinking, and the design process.

The project culminates in a showcase where students pitch their wearable's design, demo its warning features, and reflect on how it could uphold children's rights and safety in disaster-prone areas.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: People design for purpose

LINES OF INQUIRY: How the design thinking process helps to create in various ways (Function); Design thinking helps to meet the needs of different communities. (Causation) (Ethical and practical considerations); Effective ways to engage with an audience (Connection)

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Causation, Connection, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Workshops with UNICEF on Wed 25th June, including an introduction to the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child and a Mini Design Thinking Workshop. In Week 2, students engage in activities such as reading "Jackie French Fire," exploring nature's resilience, using a Geoscience mapping tool, and creating a class Wonder Wall with student questions. They also watch "Nature is Speaking," participate in "Step In, Step Out, Step Back," discuss provocation statements, vocabulary, and the Risk Triangle.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Jigsaw activity to deepen understanding of natural hazards.
- Bushfire focus: "See, Think, Wonder" on "The Summer Australia Burned," review of bushfire infographics, mapping bushfire risk using Geoscience Australia tools, and analysis of infrastructure and risk.
- Earthquake focus: National Geographic video, reading comprehension, Moment Magnitude Scale, class definition of an earthquake, Tectonic Plates Jigsaw Puzzle, and optional ClickView video.
- Floods focus: Compare before and after of Lismore, watch "Understanding Floods" and "Flooding Aftermath" videos, create Flood Survival Posters, and explore economic impacts.
- Sorting out: Students form groups, watch a UNICEF briefing video, choose a disaster topic and child right, delegate research roles, create topographical maps, analyze impacts, and develop empathy maps.
- Design process: Individual brainstorms, Crazy 8s, storyboards, team storyboards, prototyping, refining, and pitch writing.
- Final presentations: Pitching and presenting projects using UNICEF marking criteria, reflecting on design success, and selecting top teams for a semi-final.

ASSESSMENT: Prototype and pitch presentations, information reports, graphs representing demographic and historical data, Seesaw portfolio tasks, UNICEF marking guide and checklist, formative assessments (e.g., letter writing, cause and effect wheels), and reflection activities.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong transdisciplinary connections: Visual Art (designing UNICEF-inspired logos, printmaking, digital art), Maths (data analysis, measurement, mapping), Science (natural hazards, human impact), Geography (mapping, bushfire/flood impacts), and French (presenting plays, poetry, calligrammes).
- Action opportunities: Students design solutions for real-world problems, focusing on empathy and needs.
- Technology integration: Use of iPads for digital art, mapping tools, Seesaw for portfolios, ClickView for videos, and digital tools for data analysis and presentations.
- Teacher reflections highlight the need for more time in the prototype phase and suggest moving some content to other units for clarity.
- Emphasis on student agency: Students choose topics, roles, and solutions, and engage in self- and peer-assessment.
- Opportunities for further development: Strengthen empathy connections, review timeline and teaching sequence for future years, and consider the balance between formal pitches and design process focus.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways environmental stewardship can drive change

s.d: Students use Minecraft Education to design and build a future-focused, sustainable version of Melbourne, integrating real-world environmental stewardship and community values. They must justify their design choices based on local council initiatives and conservation needs, then present their virtual city to the class as a persuasive pitch for change.

Students create persuasive digital campaigns (posters, infographics, or short videos) advocating for a local environmental initiative inspired by Monash Council programs. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to integrate photos from their nature walk and overlay key conservation messages.

Students collaboratively build a digital mind map of local council sustainability initiatives, linking each to its potential impact on the community and environment. They add research, photos, and reflections from their council investigation and nature walk.

Students author a digital book documenting their inquiry into First Nations stewardship, local flora and fauna, and their own conservation proposals. They combine text, images from the iPad camera, and audio reflections to demonstrate understanding of community-environment interactions.

Students curate and annotate a playlist of short videos on conservation, local council actions, and environmental stewardship. They add their own video reflections explaining how each resource connects to the central idea and propose actions for their school community.

Students work in teams to design and build a miniature 'sustainable garden ecosystem' using recycled materials (cardboard, plastic bottles, fabric scraps, etc.) to create terrain, water features, and plant models. They integrate simple environmental sensors (e.g., soil moisture, light, or temperature sensors connected to a microcontroller like a BBC micro:bit or Makey Makey) to monitor garden conditions. Students craft garden signage and habitat features, then code their microcontroller to trigger physical responses?such as lighting up LEDs, activating a small buzzer, or raising a hand-crafted flag?when certain environmental thresholds are met (e.g., soil too dry, too much sun). Teams document and iterate their designs using the Design Cycle, reflecting on how their garden models represent stewardship and local conservation challenges, and how technology can help communities care for the environment.

plannerText: Students explore how communities interact with the environment through conservation, local values, and stewardship. They investigate First Nations perspectives, research local council sustainability initiatives, conduct a nature walk to observe local flora and fauna, and develop persuasive pitches for environmental action within their school community.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways in which environmental stewardship can drive change

KEY CONCEPTS: Change, Responsibility, Perspective

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students watch the film "2040" as a cohort and then draw what they think the year 2040 could look like in terms of transport, cities, schools, and fuels. They participate in a gallery walk to explain their choices. The class also watches "The Lorax" and discusses comprehension questions about community values and environmental impact. The class novel "Blueback" is introduced, and students use the 'Think, Puzzle, Explore' thinking routine to respond to the central idea and lines of inquiry.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Engage with texts about First Nations connection to Country, including picture books ("Welcome to Country," "Caring for Country," "Looking After Country with Fire"), AITSAS map, and Wurundjeri season posters.
- Visit and learn from guest speaker Scott Darlow (noted as a visit on 21st May).
- Research local flora and fauna using the Monash Gardens for Wildlife brochure and conduct a nature walk around the school to observe and photograph native plants and animals.
- Compare findings with council guides and discuss conservation.
- Investigate local council initiatives for environmental sustainability and create mind maps of these initiatives.
- Write a pitch for implementing a chosen council initiative at school.
- Create and present persuasive presentations/advertisements for their initiative to classmates.
- Plan and share advertisements using a planning template.
- Suggested future excursions: Alex Wilkie Nature Reserve and Monash Council visit (noted in teacher reflections).

ASSESSMENT: 
- Poster creation using the Generate, Sort, Connect, Elaborate thinking routine.
- Nature walk observations and comparison with council guides.
- Mind map of local council initiatives.
- Written pitch for a school-based environmental initiative.
- Persuasive presentation/advertisement pitched to peers and teachers.
- Portfolio tasks and class discussions.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include implementing local council sustainability initiatives at school.
- Transdisciplinary connections: Strong links to Australian Curriculum sustainability priorities, First Nations perspectives, and ethical research skills.
- Integration with IT: Students use iPads for photographing wildlife, creating digital presentations, and planning advertisements.
- Guest speaker (Scott Darlow) and possible excursions (Alex Wilkie Nature Reserve, Monash Council).
- Emphasis on student agency through choice of initiative and collaborative work.
- Opportunities for digital learning coach: Support with digital mind mapping, multimedia presentations, and collaborative platforms for sharing pitches and advertisements.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: The discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations.
lo: Significance of resource discovery in development of societies; Impact of migration on cultural, social and economic growth; Exploration and settlement effects on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students research a key resource discovery (e.g., gold, wool, wheat, or minerals) that shaped Australia's growth, then use Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions to design and build a 3D model of the invention or technology that enabled its extraction or use. Students present their build as a virtual museum exhibit, explaining its historical impact on Australia's development.

Students create interactive digital timelines using Canva, mapping out major resource discoveries in Australia and their ripple effects on migration, Indigenous communities, and economic growth. Each event can include images, short videos, and student-written summaries to deepen understanding of cause and effect.

Students collaboratively build a Padlet 'Migration Wall' where they post digital artefacts (photos, maps, quotes, short audio clips) representing different migration waves to Australia, linking each to the discovery of resources and the resulting cultural, social, and economic changes.

Students use Explain Everything to create animated explainer videos that unpack the push and pull factors of migration during the Gold Rush and other resource booms, integrating maps, voiceovers, and annotated diagrams to show how resource discovery transformed Australian society.

Students curate and annotate a playlist of historical video clips from Clickview, focusing on the Gold Rush, Eureka Stockade, and other pivotal moments. They add their own commentary and discussion prompts to highlight how these events shaped the nation's growth and identity.

s.t: Gold Rush Transport Tech: Interactive Migration Journeys
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build physical model transport vehicles (e.g., wagons, steamships, or pack animals) using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, representing how people migrated during Australia's resource booms. They then embed simple programmable robotics (such as Sphero Indi or Bee-Bots) into their vehicles, coding them to follow a custom-built migration route map that includes hand-crafted landscape features (rivers, goldfields, towns, Indigenous lands). Along the route, students create interactive 'event stations' (pop-up info cards, movable barriers, or decision points) highlighting historical challenges (e.g., crossing rivers, negotiating with local populations, resource scarcity). Teams iterate using the Design Cycle: empathize with historical figures, define migration challenges, ideate transport solutions, prototype vehicles and routes, test robotic journeys, and reflect on how resource discovery shaped migration and impacted diverse communities.

plannerText: Students explore how the discovery of resources such as gold, wool, and wheat impacted the growth and development of Australia, with a focus on migration, economic change, and effects on Indigenous and colonial populations. Activities include historical research, case studies, timelines, perspective-taking, and hands-on prototyping to understand the significance of resource discovery in shaping societies.
