plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The production of goods; The exchange of currency for goods and services; Ethics in the marketplace.

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Connection, Economics, Supply and demand, Distribution.

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students define, connect, and extend business and financial terminology by writing meanings under words they know, drawing linking lines between connected words, and creating a mind map of the central idea. They consider the lines of inquiry and use paired fluency to read a text and answer questions. There is also a focus on sustainability games such as The Fish Game, Power Our Community, Stop a Disaster, and Journey 2050 to introduce concepts of sustainability and systems thinking.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Mind mapping and vocabulary activities to connect business concepts.
- Playing sustainability simulation games (The Fish Game, Power Our Community, Stop a Disaster, Journey 2050).
- Supply & Demand activities.
- Types of Resources ? Sam's Soccer Ball Factory activity.
- Creating production chains for a chosen product, showing all stages from raw materials to retail.
- Watching BTN clips about cost of living and inflation, completing cause and effect worksheets.
- Collaborative research and role play on ethics in the marketplace.
- Launch Suburb project: students design a suburb (name, logo, floor plan), vote on names, set up jobs, write job applications, and manage roles.
- Setting up student businesses, writing business proposals, trading goods and services, and using receipts.
- City Trading Launch: students participate in trading sessions across three suburbs.
- Ongoing roles and business management within homeroom time.
- Use of spreadsheets and OneNote for budgeting and business tracking.
- Maths Probability Carnival and Financial Maths Investigation workshops.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring spreadsheet use (scheduled and surprise checks).
- Weekly meetings to debrief progress in roles.
- Reconciling receipts with spreadsheets.
- Reviewing business proposals.
- Financial Maths Investigation & Workshops.
- Maths Probability Carnival.
- Portfolio Tasks ? HWOO Evaluation / Reflection, Production Chain, Job Application Letter.
- Seesaw Task - Unit Evaluation.
- Peer and self-assessment through classroom discussions, town and city meetings, and analysis of business profit and loss.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Maths (financial maths, probability), English (job applications, business plans), and digital technologies (spreadsheets, OneNote).
- Opportunities for student agency: students choose business ideas, jobs, and roles; design and run their own businesses; manage budgets; and enact roles in a simulated economy.
- Technology integration includes spreadsheets for budgeting and business tracking, OneNote for documentation, and Seesaw for reflection and assessment.
- Emphasis on ethics, collaboration, and self-management skills.
- Students are encouraged to bring in props and create businesses at home, connecting classroom learning to real-life experiences.
- Transdisciplinary connections to Civics and Citizenship, Economics and Business, Social Studies, and Digital Technologies.
- Opportunities for action include creating and running businesses, reflecting on ethical decisions, and managing personal spending and saving.

ci: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society
lo: How inventions have changed over time to meet the demands of society; The UN Sustainable Development Goals and the global problems they address; Using the design thinking process to create innovations that respond to real-world needs

s.d: Students design a 3D interactive prototype of their invention using Merge Cubes, then use Canva's AI image generation to create futuristic visual overlays and AR labels. This app-smash allows students to bring their SDG-inspired inventions to life, letting peers and families explore their models in augmented reality at the Future Thinkers' Forum.

Create a collaborative digital 'Invention Timeline' Padlet where students post images and short videos of inventions they've researched, annotated with how each responded to changing societal needs. Students can comment on each other's posts, sparking discussion about innovation and transformation.

Students explore the virtual museum of key innovations, then design and build their own SDG-inspired invention in Minecraft, justifying how it addresses a current global need. This links directly to the unit's focus on inventions as responses to societal change.

Students document their design process in a multimedia digital journal using Book Creator, combining photos of prototypes, voice recordings explaining their thinking, and reflections on how their invention addresses their chosen SDG.
Students use Forms to gather feedback from peers, teachers, and families on their invention prototypes, analysing the data to refine their designs and better meet the needs of their target audience.

s.t: SDG Solutions Arcade: Interactive Invention Stations
s.d: Students select a real-world problem linked to a UN Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) and use the Design Cycle to invent a creative solution. First, they research and brainstorm, then sketch and plan their idea. Using recycled materials, cardboard, motors, LEDs, and craft supplies, students build a physical prototype of their invention. To bring it to life, they integrate a Sphero Indi or similar simple robot as a 'smart helper'?for example, coding it to activate a feature, demonstrate a process, or simulate a function within their invention (such as transporting water, sorting waste, or delivering supplies). Each invention is displayed as an interactive station at the Future Thinkers' Forum, where visitors can physically interact with the prototype and trigger its digital/robotic features to see the SDG solution in action.

plannerText: Students investigate how inventions and initiatives are responses to changing societal needs, exploring past and present innovations, analysing how inventions evolve, and using the design process to create their own SDG-inspired prototypes. Activities include research, science lab experiments, collaborative design thinking, and presenting inventions at the Future Thinkers' Forum.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The needs of society change over time; Inventions are a copy, transformation or combination of existing ideas; People can problem solve to address needs in society; The design process and scientific method.

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Change, Innovation, Transformation, Society, Design.

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students look at different inventions of the past and answer questions about them (name, year/decade, purpose, evidence for their hypothesis). They then break down the central idea by highlighting significant words, brainstorming, and connecting images to these words.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- "What's That Invention?" activity (analysing past inventions)
- Breaking down the central idea with images and brainstorming
- "Changed Over Time": Students choose a modern invention, research its history and changes, and describe significant changes over time with images
- Science lab sessions: Exploring elements of inventions (Light Boxes and Steam experiments)
- SDGs: Ordering, grouping, and unpacking activities
- Watching "Everything is a Remix" and analysing ideas
- SDGs further investigation: Students choose 3 SDGs to research using infographics and tables
- "Shark Tank": Analysis of ideas
- Following the Design Process in groups (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test), using PowerPoint and Canva AI for digital designs
- Guest speaker: Anna Hancock (expert in Climate Change) gives a presentation
- FTF (Future Thinkers' Forum): Students present their inventions and receive guest feedback

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring student articulation of SDG priorities and understanding of global issues
- Checklist completion
- Invention timelines
- Parent feedback from the FTF
- Portfolio tasks, presentations at the Future Thinkers' Forum, self and peer assessment during the forum

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong focus on student agency: Students select SDGs, form groups based on interests, choose problems and design solutions, and present in various formats (digital/physical)
- Integration of digital tools: Canva AI, PowerPoint, Seesaw for documentation and reflection
- Transdisciplinary connections to science (lab sessions), SDGs, and design thinking
- Opportunities for action: Students share research websites, design inventions for real-world problems, and present to the community
- Noted need for more time with technology (3D printing, VR) in future iterations
- Resources include UN SDGs, YouTube (Shark Tank, BrainPOP), library research tools, picture story books, and guest speakers

(Extracted from pages 1?6, including both text and referenced resources/activities.)

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for conservation and environmental stewardship; Ways in which First Nations land management practices can drive positive environmental change

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of local environments or endangered habitats using Merge Cubes and the iPad camera. They record short video explanations of stewardship actions, then use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to overlay themselves as digital guides within their virtual environments. This immersive, hands-on workflow brings conservation and land management to life, allowing students to 'step into' their own digital habitats.

Set up a collaborative digital wall where students post photos, videos, and reflections from their exploration of local environments, native gardens, and stewardship activities. They can map connections between Indigenous and non-Indigenous perspectives, and crowdsource ideas for conservation action.

Students author digital books documenting their journey through the unit, integrating research on habitats, stewardship, and Indigenous land management. They can embed audio interviews, photos from excursions, and their own conservation pledges.

Students virtually step into the shoes of wildlife rangers, exploring how science and community action protect habitats and species. They learn about conservation roles and design digital solutions for real-world environmental challenges.

Students design visually impactful conservation campaign posters or infographics, using Canva's templates and 'Remove Background' tool to highlight local stewardship efforts and Indigenous perspectives. These can be shared digitally or displayed around the school to inspire action.

s.t: Guardians of the Biome: Interactive Ecosystem Terrariums
s.d: Students work in teams to research a specific world biome (e.g., rainforest, savannah, tundra, desert) and design a physical terrarium model using recycled containers, natural materials, clay, and craft supplies to represent the biome's unique features, flora, and fauna. They then integrate a simple programmable microcontroller (e.g., BBC micro:bit or Makey Makey) with sensors (moisture, light, temperature) and basic circuits to simulate and monitor environmental conditions inside their terrarium. Students program the microcontroller to trigger visual or audio alerts (LEDs, buzzers, or iPad audio cues) when biome health indicators (like soil moisture or light) move out of balance, encouraging stewardship actions. The project follows the Design Cycle: empathise (with living things in the biome), define (key environmental challenges), ideate (creative terrarium solutions), prototype (build and code), test (simulate changes), and reflect (share findings and stewardship strategies).

plannerText: This unit immerses students in the concept of stewardship, exploring both Indigenous and non-Indigenous connections to land, conservation, and environmental responsibility. Activities include mapping world biomes, writing emails to local trees, investigating land management practices, and hands-on stewardship projects in the school garden, all with a strong focus on action and student agency.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Different people's connection to country influences their perspectives and responsibilities towards the environment.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Different people's connection to country (European and indigenous); Animals, themselves and indigenous connections to the land; That they have a responsibility to conserve the environment and make change; How they can conserve different parts of the environment; What the features of different environments are; What people are doing to drive change and how they can be a part of that.

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, Perspective, Responsibility, Conservation, Environment, Stewardship

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Unpacking the Central Idea; Gallery Walk; Vocabulary - Environments. Students unpack the central idea, participate in a gallery walk to observe and discuss images or information about environments, and engage in vocabulary activities related to environments.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Exploring different environments using worksheets and fact sheets.
- Investigating conservation and stewardship, including an Indigenous focus with lessons on Indigenous land management.
- Writing emails to trees as part of the Melbourne Urban Forest initiative.
- Chum Creek & ECOSS Visit (excursion to learn about stewardship and conservation in practice).
- Mapping world biomes and examining native gardens and species at Wesley College.
- Watching clips about First Peoples' action in caring for the land (e.g., traditional burning).

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring understanding of indigenous connections to land and country, unpacking vocabulary, and observing native gardens.
- Documenting through mapping tasks of world biomes, camp booklets (Seesaw ? Portfolio task), and writing emails to trees.
- Measuring individual understanding of key terms (management, conservation, stewardship) and learning in Seesaw tasks about changing the natural environment, reasons for conservation, and human impact.
- Feedback given via Seesaw; ongoing formative assessment on class activities.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include student-initiated improvements to gardens around the Year 5 'Rose Garden' and collaboration with Environment Prefects for native planting.
- Transdisciplinary connections with Science, Geography, History, Civics and Citizenship, Social Studies, and Design and Technologies.
- Integration of digital tools such as Seesaw for portfolio tasks and reflections, and use of the Melbourne Urban Forest website for research and email activities.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment through poster creation, reflection on responsibilities to the land, and sharing perspectives of First Nations Peoples.
- Suggestion for future years: expand activities around how First Peoples cared for and continue to care for the land.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has had a lasting impact on different countries and their people
lo: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; How colonisation transformed society and culture; The responsibility of colonisers and the perspectives of those colonised

s.d: Students create interactive 3D Merge Cube experiences that allow users to explore different perspectives of colonisation?such as the viewpoint of Indigenous peoples, colonisers, and contemporary Australians. Students then app smash by embedding their Merge Cube AR videos and reflections into a multimedia Book Creator eBook, presenting a layered, immersive narrative of colonisation's impact.

Launch a collaborative digital timeline where students post research, images, and primary sources about colonisation in Australia and other countries. Each post can include student voice recordings, artwork, or short video reflections, allowing for a rich, multi-modal exploration of the central idea.

Students design persuasive infographics or digital posters that highlight the lasting impacts of colonisation on different countries and their people. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to create striking visuals that juxtapose pre- and post-colonisation imagery.

Curate a playlist of documentary clips and historical re-enactments about colonisation. Students use ClickView's interactive video features to annotate, ask questions, and respond to key moments, deepening their understanding of causation and change.

Students explore the museum of key innovations from history, then research and build their own 'inventions' that either resulted from or responded to colonisation. This activity encourages students to consider how technological and cultural changes were shaped by, and in turn shaped, colonisation.

s.t: Colonisation Contraptions: Cause & Effect Chain Reactions
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build large-scale Rube Goldberg-style machines that physically represent a sequence of events and impacts triggered by colonisation?such as arrival, settlement, resource extraction, cultural changes, and resistance. Using recycled materials, cardboard, marbles, pulleys, and levers, each group constructs a section illustrating a key moment or consequence. To integrate tech, students embed simple programmable elements (e.g., using micro:bit or Makey Makey with conductive materials) that trigger sounds, lights, or digital messages at critical points in their chain reaction, symbolising voices or perspectives (e.g., Indigenous, colonisers, environment). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise with different perspectives, define the sequence of events, ideate mechanisms, prototype and test their contraption, then reflect on how each action leads to the next?making visible the complex, interconnected impacts of colonisation.

plannerText: Students explore the reasons, methods, and impacts of colonisation through activities such as perspective-taking with 'The Rabbits', research projects on British and global colonisation, and oral presentations. The unit emphasises empathy, critical thinking, and student agency as learners investigate historical and contemporary effects of colonisation on societies and cultures.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; Transformation of society & culture due to colonisation; The impact of the choices of colonisers and those colonised.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; Transformation of society & culture due to colonisation; The impact of the choices of colonisers and those colonised.

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Change, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: After reading "The Rabbits," students use a sunglasses template to draw and write from the perspective of different groups involved in colonisation. They answer questions about motivations, experiences, changes, and responses. Students also break down the central idea by highlighting significant words, brainstorming, and connecting images to the central idea.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Reading and perspective activity using "The Rabbits" and a sunglasses template.
- Breaking down the central idea with brainstorming and image connections.
- Research and summarising: Watching an Australian History video and completing a template.
- Creating a class mini SAT example on Britain and individual research templates.
- Using ClickView resources on the impact of colonisation on Indigenous Australians.
- Conducting research on Britain using a provided link.
- Identifying modern examples of colonisation/invasion (e.g., World War II, Israel-Palestine).
- Making connections between the class novel "Convict Boy" and the unit of inquiry, as well as Tom Appleby's memories.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring understanding of the Central Idea and key concepts.
- Documenting learning with a mini SAT on Britain.
- Measuring understanding through a Seesaw Task as SAT.
- Oral presentations of research with peer assessment and feedback.
- Reflection on final tasks, identifying areas of success and development.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Library resources accessed via WiSE tile; library sessions focus on research skills and accessing suitable websites.
- Students have choice in which country to research and present about, supporting agency.
- Students create their own PowerPoint and oral presentation, with peer feedback.
- Student-initiated action includes discussions about current political unrest and questioning colonisation vs invasion (e.g., Russian invasion of Ukraine, Gaza-Israel conflict, USA bombing of Iran).
- Opportunities for technology integration include using Seesaw for assessment, PowerPoint for presentations, and ClickView for video resources. Library sessions support digital research skills.

ci: Values, beliefs and actions contribute to a diverse society
lo: How our values and beliefs shape our identity and interactions; Our responsibility to manage emotions and be safe online and in person; The importance of resilience, respect and positive relationships in a diverse community

s.d: Students design and script AI-powered chatbots that simulate conversations about values, beliefs, and ethical dilemmas. Using Wise Discussion Chatbots, groups can create branching dialogue scenarios where users must make choices reflecting different perspectives, fostering empathy and critical thinking about diversity and inclusion.

Students collaboratively create digital infographics or posters that visually represent the diverse values, beliefs, and actions within their class or community. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to incorporate their own photos and symbols, making the work highly personalised and visually engaging.

Each student authors a digital page in a shared Book Creator project, sharing a personal story or family tradition that reflects their values or beliefs. The final digital book becomes a class anthology celebrating diversity, which can be shared with families and the wider school community.

Students contribute to a collaborative Padlet wall, posting multimedia reflections (text, images, voice notes) about how their actions and beliefs shape their identity and contribute to a diverse society. This living wall can be used for peer feedback and to spark classroom discussions about similarities and differences.

Small groups plan, record, and edit short podcasts where they interview each other or community members about how values and beliefs influence actions. Students learn to script, record, and edit audio, then publish their podcasts for the school community to listen and respond.

Students work in teams to design and build large, collaborative sculptures representing diverse identities, values, or beliefs using recycled materials, cardboard, fabric, and clay. Each sculpture features embedded simple circuits (using Makey Makey, micro:bit, or similar) and conductive materials, so when touched, different parts trigger digital audio stories, empathy prompts, or sound effects recorded by students on iPads. Teams must prototype, test, and refine both the physical structure and the interactive tech, ensuring the sculptures invite classmates to learn about and empathise with others' perspectives through touch-activated experiences.

plannerText: The unit explores students' awareness of self and others, focusing on empathy, respect, and understanding of diverse values and beliefs. Activities include CyberSafety lessons, social-emotional learning (URStrong, Grow Your Mind), collaborative discussions, and creative reflection tasks to help students recognise their own and others' contributions to a diverse society.

After the chat, students create a personal action plan outlining strategies for managing emotions and building respectful online communities.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express","d":"Students design visually striking digital posters or infographics that showcase the importance of respect, inclusion, and understanding in a diverse society, using Adobe Express's templates and 'Remove Background' feature for creative self-portraits.","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Using the Design Cycle, students work in teams to prototype a 'Respect Robot' with Lego Spike Prime. The robot must perform a task that symbolically represents inclusion or empathy (e.g., delivering messages of kindness or bridging two groups), requiring students to ideate, build, test, and refine their solution while reflecting on how technology can promote positive actions in a diverse community.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Wellbeing is influenced by the connections we have with ourselves and others.

LINES OF INQUIRY: 
- The connection between beliefs, values and actions;
- How we show respect for ourselves and others;
- Strategies for building positive relationships.

KEY CONCEPTS: Responsibility, Form, Connection, Identity, Relationships, Safety

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Essential Agreement, t-shirt about me, "Find someone who..." Bingo, GYM-Meet the animals in your brain.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- 16 lessons of CyberSafety
- 11 UR Strong lessons
- 5 Grow Your Mind lessons
- Seesaw Tasks: CyberSafety - Create your own golden rule, GYM - Sharing problems, Seesaw Evaluation
- Activities include: Essential Agreement, t-shirt about me, Bingo, GYM-Meet the animals in your brain, group activities, class discussions, roleplay options, goal setting, and reflection tasks.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Monitoring: Learner Profile attributes when self-assessing collaborative tasks.
- Documenting: ATL and Learner Profile certificates given at assembly.
- Goal Setting: portfolio task, morning routines, desk tags for reminders, portfolio feedback review.
- Measuring: Portfolio Task (reflection), Seesaw Tasks (Sharing problems, CyberSafety - Golden Rule, Evaluation).

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities: Students used strategies from the programs to resolve their own issues, discussed online difficulties with trusted adults, and shared knowledge of online scams.
- Transdisciplinary connections: Wellbeing at Wesley, Cyber Safety, URStrong, Grow Your Mind, Health and PE, Chinese, French, Choral, English, Mathematics, Civics and Citizenship, Social Studies, Design and Technologies.
- Integration notes: Weekly sessions and group activities using URSTRONG, CyberSafety, Grow Your Mind; Seesaw used for assessment and reflection; class assemblies and collaborative tasks for student voice and ownership; technology used for online safety awareness and digital portfolio tasks.

ci: Understanding global issues and using effective communication empowers us to raise awareness and inspire action.
lo: Global issues are caused by interconnected factors that require collective solutions (Causation) ??? Presentations in the media can reflect the perspective of the author rather than the truth (Perspective) ??? We must make responsible choices when responding to and creating media (Responsibility) ??? Messages can be expressed through a range of mediums and elements can be adjusted to enhance effectiveness (Communication) ??? Design choices contribute to the effectiveness of a message (Influence)

s.d: Students create a global issues podcast series, using Adobe Express Podcasting to script, record, and edit episodes that raise awareness and inspire action on their chosen issue. They can incorporate interviews, sound effects, and music to enhance the impact and reach a wider audience.

Students design a persuasive digital media campaign using Canva, creating infographics, posters, and social media graphics that communicate key facts and calls to action about their global issue. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create professional visuals without a green screen.

Groups collaboratively author an interactive digital book in Book Creator, combining text, images, audio, and video to tell the story of their global issue, highlight solutions, and showcase student action. The book can be published and shared with the school community.

s.t: Minecraft: Peter Is Here Ai For Cultural Heritage
s.d: Students explore how technology can be used to protect and celebrate cultural heritage sites, then build a Minecraft world that raises awareness about a global issue connected to cultural heritage, using the lesson 'Peter Is Here Ai For Cultural Heritage'.

Students curate a collaborative Padlet wall to collect and share resources, campaign ideas, action plans, and reflections on their global issue. They can embed videos, images, and links, fostering a digital hub for their advocacy work.

Students work in teams to select a global issue they are passionate about. Using the Design Cycle, they design and build a large, physical 'storybox'?a decorated, multi-chambered structure made from recycled cardboard, wood, and craft materials. Inside each chamber, students embed simple interactive tech (like Makey Makey or micro:bit sensors) connected to audio modules or LED displays. When visitors touch or interact with different parts of the storybox, they trigger student-created digital stories, soundscapes, or messages (recorded on tablets and uploaded to the device) that share personal narratives, facts, or calls to action related to their chosen issue. The final exhibition invites the community to explore, listen, and engage with the students' creative advocacy in a multisensory, hands-on experience.

plannerText: This Year 6 HWEO unit centres on students investigating global issues, developing a central idea and lines of inquiry, and taking action through a PYP Exhibition. Students research, collaborate, and communicate their findings using a variety of media, culminating in a campaign or performance to raise awareness and inspire action within the community.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Not specified. (The planner describes the Exhibition process and inquiry into global issues, media, and expression, but does not state a single central idea for the unit.)

LINES OF INQUIRY: Not specified. (Students create their own central ideas and lines of inquiry as part of the Exhibition process; no fixed lines are listed for the unit as a whole.)

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Perspective, Responsibility, Communication, Influence

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
- Understanding the purpose and benefit of the Exhibition: Students revisit and reflect on what Exhibition is, what it looks like, and its purpose.
- Exploring different AI uses and how these can help learning: AI session led by the DLP Team.
- Guided teaching of Root Core Analysis using a global problem/issue.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Creating vision statements and central ideas in groups.
- Watching the 2024 Elsternwick video for inspiration.
- Compass Sustainable Model and brainstorm mapping.
- Unpacking transdisciplinary themes, learner profile attributes, and ATL skills; group poster creation.
- Introduction to the MISO method of research.
- Upskilling in Canva, CoSpaces, podcast audio editing, and PowerPoint.
- Researching lines of inquiry, using library databases and articles, creating bibliographies.
- Creating PYP Exhibition logos and uploading to Seesaw.
- Identifying and interviewing experts.
- Designing and implementing an action (e.g., advocacy campaign, lifestyle change, media project).
- Script development, rehearsal, and green screen filming.
- Live presentation of performance components and keynote presentations.
- Presenting media campaigns (live and pre-recorded/created elements).
- Guest speakers: Ben Lilley (Media Campaign), Launch Day guests, Kathryn (Library services), Andrew DelMastro (DLP support).

ASSESSMENT:
- Rubrics with success criteria in journals following the inquiry cycle.
- Ongoing teacher and student conversations.
- Music teachers record class performances; students upload compositions and work into Seesaw.
- Visual evidence and process journals.
- Self and peer assessment via Seesaw Portfolio tasks.
- Mentor feedback, teacher follow-up, and weekly student review.
- Pre- and post-self-assessment tasks with teacher feedback.
- Peer assessment of logos and performances.
- Summative assessment: live and visual presentations of Exhibition projects.

TEACHER NOTES:
- Strong emphasis on student agency: students choose their passion, form groups, create their own central ideas and lines of inquiry, and select action.
- Technology integration: Canva, CoSpaces, podcasting, PowerPoint, Seesaw, green screen, AI tools.
- Transdisciplinary connections: Drama (performance), Visual Art (logo design), LOTE (language posters and international perspectives), Music (composition and performance).
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment are embedded throughout.
- Action opportunities: students design and implement advocacy, lifestyle, or educational campaigns.
- Collaboration with mentors and guest experts.
- Reflection and feedback are ongoing and integral to the process.
- Exhibition is the culminating PYP experience, integrating research, action, and communication skills.

(Note: The Exhibition unit is highly student-driven, so many elements (central idea, lines of inquiry, action) are co-constructed and individualized by each group.)

ci: The way a country is governed impacts the lives of citizens.
lo: The key institutions and functions of Australia's democratic system of government (Function) ??? How government decisions impact citizens at federal, state and local levels (Causation) ??? The rights and responsibilities of Australian citizens and their obligations as global citizens (Equality/Citizenship)

s.d: Students create detailed annotated maps using National Geographic MapMaker to visually represent Australia's three levels of government and their geographic jurisdictions. They plot key government buildings, electoral boundaries, and community services, then layer data showing how government decisions impact citizens across regions. This interactive mapping deepens understanding of governance structures and spatial relationships in a hands-on, visual way.

Students script and record a podcast episode imagining a 'Day in the Life' of citizens living under different government systems, using Adobe Express Podcasting to combine voice narration, sound effects, and interviews. This immersive audio storytelling deepens their understanding of how government decisions impact daily life and encourages empathy by giving voice to diverse perspectives. Teachers can share the final podcasts to spark class discussions on citizenship and governance. https://podcast.adobe.com

Students explore the stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and simulate how active citizenship and government decisions can create positive change. They can build virtual monuments or scenarios that reflect the impact of governance on society.

Collaboratively build a digital debate wall where students post arguments, counterarguments, and evidence about real-world government decisions (e.g., social media bans, new laws). This interactive platform allows for peer feedback and the development of critical thinking skills.

Create dynamic, student-led video explainers or animated infographics that break down the process of how a bill becomes law in Australia. Students can use Adobe Express to storyboard, animate, and narrate the journey from idea to legislation, making civics content engaging and accessible.

Students work in teams to design, prototype, and build an original, large-scale board game that simulates how Australian government systems operate and impact citizens. Using cardboard, recycled materials, clay, and art supplies, they craft 3D game boards, tokens, and interactive elements representing government branches, roles, and real-life scenarios. Teams embed digital features by integrating simple circuits (e.g., Makey Makey or micro:bit) to trigger sound effects, lights, or digital scorekeeping on iPads when players land on certain spaces or pass legislation. Throughout the Design Cycle, students ideate game mechanics, prototype physical and digital components, test gameplay, and iterate to create an engaging, hands-on learning experience that brings government systems to life.

plannerText: In this unit, students investigate how different systems of government function, the roles and responsibilities at each level in Australia, and how government decisions impact citizens' lives. They engage in inquiry-based activities such as mapping government structures, simulating law-making, reflecting on citizenship, and proposing their own bills, culminating in creative reflections and real-world connections.

They define the problem as how to clearly share government announcements at local, state, and federal levels. They ideate by brainstorming different noticeboard designs and information layouts. Using cardboard, paper, markers, and simple craft materials, they prototype a physical noticeboard that organises information by government level and type. Finally, they test their design by presenting it to classmates for feedback on clarity and usefulness, then refine their prototype accordingly.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The way a country is governed impacts the lives of citizens.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The key institutions of Australian democratic system of government; The roles and responsibilities of Australia's 3 levels of government; The responsibilities of electors and representatives in Australia's democracy; Where ideas for new laws can come from and how they become law; The rights and responsibilities of Australian citizens; The obligations citizens may consider they may have beyond their own national borders as active and informed global citizens.

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Causation, Equality, Citizenship, Governance, Influence

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students brainstorm all the rules or systems that affect their lives (school rules, local laws, voting, taxes, transport, healthcare). They discuss who makes these rules and why they are needed. The central idea is unpacked with students, highlighting keywords (government, governed, impact, citizens). Students watch a video or presentation about Australia's states, capitals, and key locations, then label all states and territories on a map of Australia and identify each capital city.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Unpack the Central Idea and Lines of Inquiry, including a "Quick Think ? Who Makes the Rules?" activity and a map activity labeling Australian states and capitals.
- Watch and take notes from BTN and PEO videos about the 3 levels of government and Parliament.
- Brainstorm and record key vocabulary related to Parliament and government roles.
- Note-taking on the process of passing a bill in Parliament using BTN resources.
- Citizenship exploration through ClickView videos and reading comprehension tasks about Australian citizenship.
- "We Are Australian" activity exploring belonging, diversity, and shared identity.
- Local Government Task: Students research their local government area using council websites and create a PowerPoint presentation about council services, logo, and personal connections.
- Table group discussion on the pros and cons of the Australian Government's social media ban for under 16s, using BTN and news resources.
- Bill to Parliament project: Students plan and research a bill they would like to propose, following steps in a provided PowerPoint.
- Canberra Educational Trip: Students reflect on their trip and record their thoughts on Seesaw.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing note-taking during lessons, videos, and presentations.
- Participation and listening during the Canberra trip.
- Reflection tasks in UOI books and on Seesaw.
- Canberra booklet and Seesaw task reflections.
- Summative task: A series of questions assessing key knowledge, combined with a unit evaluation and guiding questions around 'Action'.
- Self and peer assessment opportunities, including Seesaw Portfolio tasks and reflections after the Canberra trip.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Student agency is supported through voice (reflections in Canberra booklet, contributions to discussions), choice (selection of resources, tools, and examples for learning, decisions about reflections and projects, design of a bill), and ownership (students owned their views and votes in class elections and referendums).
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment are embedded, including Seesaw Portfolio tasks and reflections.
- Resources include Australian Parliament House, Australian Electoral Commission, Parliamentary Education Office, Museum of Australian Democracy, Australian War Memorial, National Capital Exhibition Program, High Court of Australia, class novel "Can You See Me," BTN clips, and AEC resources.
- Technology integration includes Seesaw for reflections and portfolio tasks, PowerPoint for presentations, and online resources for research.
- Excursion to Canberra and related reflection tasks provide real-world connections.
- Teacher reflection notes that the previous Tri-Chart Summative Assessment was replaced with a more focused summative task and unit evaluation. Students also created and presented their own Bills to Parliament based on issues they were passionate about.

ci: The relationships between the Earth, Moon and Sun create predictable patterns that shape life on our planet and are understood through both scientific and cultural perspectives.
lo: The movement of the Earth, Moon and Sun causes predictable patterns such as day/night, moon phases, tides and seasons (Causation) ??? Different cultures, including Indigenous knowledge systems, interpret the night sky in unique and meaningful ways (Perspective) ??? The Earth, Moon and Sun have specific structures, positions and relationships that can be represented through models and simulations (Form)

s.d: Students investigate the predictable patterns created by the Earth, Moon, and Sun by using Sky Map to observe and identify celestial bodies and constellations visible from their location. They connect scientific concepts like rotation and phases of the Moon with cultural stories by mapping night sky features, deepening their understanding of how different cultures interpret the same sky phenomena.

Students use 'Minecraft: The Scientific Method' to design and run virtual experiments that model the effects of Earth's rotation, revolution, and the Moon's phases. They collect data, make predictions, and compare their findings to both scientific and Indigenous perspectives on celestial patterns.

Students script and record science podcasts using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), explaining the relationships between the Earth, Moon, and Sun from both scientific and cultural perspectives. They interview classmates, share Indigenous stories, and use sound effects to bring astronomical concepts to life, culminating in a class podcast series that makes complex ideas accessible and engaging. This project empowers students to communicate their learning creatively and reach a wider audience.

Students author digital books that combine diagrams, time-lapse photos (taken with iPad camera), and narrative text to explain the relationships between the Earth, Moon, and Sun. They include sections comparing scientific and Indigenous knowledge, and embed student-created illustrations or animations.

Students collaborate on a Padlet wall to crowdsource observations of moon phases, sunrise/sunset times, and cultural stories from their families. This shared digital space becomes a living archive of both scientific data and diverse perspectives, supporting inquiry and reflection.

Students work in teams to design and build a physical shadow theatre using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. They construct movable models of the Earth, Moon, and Sun, mounted on rods or tracks, to accurately demonstrate phases of the moon, eclipses, and the changing position of shadows throughout the day and year. Students integrate micro:bit sensors or iPad stop-motion capture to record and measure shadow lengths and angles as they manipulate their models, collecting real data for analysis. The final theatre includes creative backdrops inspired by both scientific diagrams and Indigenous storytelling, and each group scripts and performs a short shadow play explaining a phenomenon (e.g., eclipse, season change) from both scientific and cultural perspectives, using their hybrid physical-digital stage.

plannerText: Students explore the relationships between the Earth, Moon, and Sun through hands-on models, time-lapse videos, moon diaries, and cultural stories. They investigate scientific concepts like rotation, tides, and seasons, compare Indigenous and scientific perspectives, and synthesize their learning through creative projects such as podcasts, diagrams, and choice-board activities.

This project empowers students to communicate their learning creatively and reach a wider audience.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Students author digital books that combine diagrams, time-lapse photos (taken with iPad camera), and narrative text to explain the relationships between the Earth, Moon, and Sun. plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Not specified.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Not specified.

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Perspective, Form

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students watch a time-lapse video of Earth's rotation and sunrise from space ("Earth from Space: Time-lapse of Earth's Rotation and Sunrise" - https://youtu.be/GOAEIMx39-w). They use a See?Think?Wonder thinking routine to generate questions and record wonderings.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Unpack the central idea and key vocabulary (orbit, rotation, phase, tide) with visuals and actions; create mind maps of Earth, Moon, and Sun connections.
- Use a globe and flashlight to simulate Earth's rotation; create diagrams of day and night; track sunrise/sunset for the local city.
- Explore Indigenous and European understandings of seasons using "Indigenous Seasons - Behind The News"; sentence completion tasks about Australia's climate.
- Model moon phases with Oreo cookies or a light source and ball; begin a Moon Diary to track phases over two weeks.
- Watch a BBC animation on tides; discuss types of tides and connect to moon phases; examine local tide data if possible.
- Read or view stories from Indigenous Australians and other cultures about the night sky; create stories and constellations from different cultural perspectives.
- Choice boards for student agency: students choose a space topic to research, listen to a podcast for structure, and plan their own podcast segment.
- Gallery walk or class presentations to synthesize understanding of Earth, Moon, and Sun interactions.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Formative: See?Think?Wonder routine, mind maps, annotated diagrams, Moon Diary check-ins, cause-effect diagrams, short written/visual responses, and story/constellation creation.
- Summative: Gallery walk/class presentations, podcast scripts/recordings, models, diagrams, written work, and student reflections (written, digital, or video).
- Ongoing: Conferencing, learning journals, exit tickets, rubrics for scientific understanding and inquiry skills, self- and peer-assessment checklists, and reflection prompts.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Digital Technologies (podcast creation, simulations, Scratch projects).
- Transdisciplinary links to Chinese (seasons/weather), French, English, Mathematics, History, Geography, Social Studies, Science, and Design and Technologies.
- Opportunities for student agency: students choose inquiry paths, formats for demonstrating learning, and set personal goals.
- Action opportunities: students encouraged to take action based on learning (e.g., sharing knowledge, creating media).
- Indigenous perspectives are included through astronomy and seasons.
- Technology suggestions: podcasting tools, digital mind mapping, data tracking apps, simulations, and Scratch for modeling concepts.
- Teacher reflection notes the importance of starting the unit on time and suggests linking to Aboriginal dreamtime stories or reconsidering camp stories to connect with the unit.

ci: Peoples' actions affect the balance of nature.
lo: What the environment is comprised of and how ecosystems function (Form) ??? Our responsibility to care for and protect the natural world (Responsibility) ??? The interdependence between humans, animals and the environment (Interdependence) ??? The consequences of human actions on the balance of nature (Consequences)

s.d: Students program Micro:bit devices to create interactive digital displays that raise awareness about endangered species and conservation efforts. They design sensors and simple games that simulate environmental threats like habitat loss or pollution, demonstrating how human actions disrupt ecosystems and exploring ways technology can support balance in nature.

Students explore how bees' needs and human actions affect the balance of nature by building 'beetopias' in Minecraft Education. They apply their understanding of interdependence and environmental responsibility by designing thriving bee habitats and reflecting on the consequences of human impact.

Students create persuasive digital campaigns or infographics to raise awareness about endangered species and the effects of human actions on the environment. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create visually striking posters that inspire action and empathy.

Students capture photos and videos during their excursions to natural sites and zoos, then use iMovie to create compelling documentaries that illustrate how human actions impact ecosystems. They edit footage to include narration and captions, producing a polished video that communicates their understanding of the interdependence between humans, animals, and the environment.

Students create a podcast series using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com) to document their STEM design projects for endangered species and share interviews with conservation experts. They plan, script, and record episodes that highlight their research, design process, and reflections on how people's actions affect the balance of nature. This authentic audio storytelling experience gives students a platform to advocate for environmental action and connect with a real-world audience.

s.t: Endangered Species Habitat Rescue AR Diorama
s.d: Students select an endangered species and use recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and natural elements to physically construct a detailed diorama of its natural habitat, including key threats and conservation features. Using iPads and the free app 'CoSpaces Edu', students then overlay their physical diorama with custom-created Augmented Reality (AR) elements?such as animated animals, digital weather effects, and interactive conservation facts?by placing AR markers within their build. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate species needs, ideate habitat solutions, prototype their hybrid diorama, test and refine both the physical and AR layers, and present their immersive rescue habitat to peers and community, explaining how their design supports species survival and raises awareness.

plannerText: The Year 6 Sharing the Planet unit focuses on how people's actions affect the balance of nature, with students investigating endangered species, conservation technologies, and Indigenous perspectives. Key activities include a STEM design project to help threatened species, excursions to zoos and natural sites, and the use of digital tools to research, design, and communicate solutions for environmental challenges.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Peoples' actions affect the balance of nature.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The environment and its components; The impact of human actions on the environment; How technology can support conservation.

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Responsibility, Interdependence, Insight, Consequences

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students unpack the lines of inquiry using a PowerPoint, write down what each line of inquiry is about in their own words, make connections to humans, animals, and the environment, and discuss their ideas with a partner or small group.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Plains Wanderer & Conservation Technology: Students learn about challenges in monitoring endangered species, how technology is used in conservation, and its benefits and limitations. They watch and read the story "Borun and Tuk," locate tribes on a map, storyboard the story, and rewrite it in their own words.
- Zoo Excursion: Students observe and discuss wildlife tracking technology at the zoo, reflect on its benefits and limitations, and consider how these ideas could inspire their own designs.
- STEM Design Project: Students research and design a technology to help an Australian animal survive, create a model or diagram, share prototypes with peers for feedback, and present their model and supporting documents to the class.
- Camp Mallana: Students reflect on their experiences and observations at camp, focusing on environmental impact and land management.
- Excursion to Bairnsdale Keeping Place and Melbourne Zoo.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Camp Booklet reflections
- PowerPoint presentations from the STEM Project
- Seesaw tasks reflecting on camp and STEM Project experiences
- Peer and self-assessment rubrics during prototype presentations
- Design Thinking Reflection (PDF)
- Portfolio tasks on Seesaw

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration of STEM and inquiry-based learning, with a focus on real-world conservation issues and Indigenous perspectives (e.g., Dreamtime stories, Borun and Tuk).
- Excursions and off-campus experiences (Camp Mallana, zoo, Bairnsdale Keeping Place) are central to the unit.
- Opportunities for student agency: students choose the animal and technology for their project, take responsibility for group roles, and reflect on their learning.
- Technology integration includes PowerPoint, Seesaw for reflection and feedback, and digital design documentation.
- Teacher reflection notes the unit's success in engaging students with technology and conservation, but suggests strengthening the action component and making links to Global Sustainability Goals or the PYP Exhibition.
- Possible future improvement: consider Werribee Zoo as a new excursion location and explore links between this unit and Aboriginal stories about the sun, moon, and earth.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges, risks and opportunities.
lo: The causes for and consequences of migration (Causation) ??? The changes in societies as a result of migration (Change) ??? The impact of migration on the individual, both positive and negative (Impact) ??? Migration results in a more diverse society (Diversity)

s.d: Students will use Minecraft Education to explore, protect, and celebrate cultural heritage sites connected to migration stories. They can research a migration event or community, then collaboratively build a digital representation of a significant site or journey, reflecting on why it is important to preserve these stories and places. This connects directly to the unit's focus on migration, diversity, and impact on society.

Students create interactive digital books documenting personal or family migration stories, or those of guest speakers. They can combine text, images, audio interviews, and maps to illustrate push/pull factors, challenges, and opportunities experienced during migration, making the learning personal and multimodal.

Students conduct and record interviews with migrants or refugees (family, staff, or community members), then upload their audio/video stories to Seesaw. They can annotate with reflections on causes, impacts, and changes resulting from migration, supporting peer feedback and empathy-building.

Students design infographics or timelines visualising key migration events in Australian or world history (e.g., Gold Rush, post-war migration, refugee arrivals). They can use Canva's templates and 'Remove Background' feature to create visually compelling, evidence-based presentations for class display.

Students collaboratively build a digital migration map using Padlet, pinning stories, photos, and facts about different migration journeys (historical or contemporary). This allows the class to visualise global migration patterns and the diversity of experiences, directly linking to the unit's central idea.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, tactile 3D map representing different migration routes from around the world using cardboard, clay, textiles, and recycled materials. Key locations feature hand-built landmarks, obstacles, and 'story stations' with QR codes. Students program Sphero Indi robots to navigate the map, simulating the journey of a migrant family. At each station, the Sphero triggers a digital audio or video narrative (recorded by students) that shares a personal migration story or historical event. The project integrates physical map-building, creative storytelling, and robotics programming, fostering empathy as students experience migration challenges and decisions through an interactive, hands-on journey.

plannerText: Students explore the causes and impacts of migration through personal narratives, interviews, and historical case studies. They create storyboards, conduct interviews, and present migration stories using a variety of media, with a strong focus on empathy, diversity, and the consequences of migration for individuals and societies.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Migration has shaped countries and individuals.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The causes for and consequences of migration; The changes in societies as a result of migration; The impact of migration on the individual (positive and negative); Migration results in a more diverse society; Key migration events (eg First fleet, gold rush, wars (WW1, WW2, Vietnam, Iraq?), government initiatives (10 pound Pom, Snowy River scheme?))

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Change, Impact, Diversity, History

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Getting to know our world geographically; Exploring key vocabulary in the central idea and lines of inquiry; Students complete a Boy Overboard Geography Research Tuning Task and use a template to explore continents and migration; Watch recommended videos such as the 7 Continents song; Use the Define Connect Extend thinking routine.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Create a storyboard about the life of Hamid and his family.
- Identify and understand the push and pull factors of why migration occurs around the world.
- KWL about understanding of refugees and migrants.
- Read the Anh Do story and rewrite his story as a biography.
- Guest speakers: Kathryn Hand and Alicia Meine share migration stories.
- Explore Australian Immigration History through infographics and videos (e.g., "A History of Immigration in Australia 1788 - Present").
- Read and discuss Cuc Lam's Story and Hamid's Story (picture books).
- Complete Push and Pull Seesaw task.
- Watch and discuss "Refugees and Migrant - BTN special" and oral histories.
- Watch video about Fariba and Fatima (refugees) and identify similarities and differences.
- Interview a migrant (homework), sort information into relevant headings, and create a plan for a biography.
- "This is your life" presentation.

ASSESSMENT: Conceptual pre- and post-assessment tasks on understanding the central idea; Anecdotal assessment of student capabilities to extract information from video clips and guest speakers; Teacher feedback on pre and post assessment tasks and work submitted via Seesaw; Verbal feedback during note-taking; Micro Lab presentations of migration stories; Seesaw portfolio tasks for self-reflection and peer assessment; Students create comprehension questions to evaluate peer understanding.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Opportunities for student agency: Students create some interview questions, choose who to interview, and families help identify interviewees. Book design and inclusion decisions are student-owned (with a template provided).
- French: Students choose an area of interest related to French-speaking countries for a project.
- Technology integration: Use of Seesaw for portfolio tasks, reflection, and submission of work; digital storytelling and presentations; video resources and online research.
- Transdisciplinary connections: Library (personal narratives), Chinese and French (language and culture), Visual Arts, Drama.
- Action opportunities: Inspire students to be internationally minded, appreciative, and accepting of how migrants and refugees have shaped Australia (advocacy, social justice, investment, participation, lifestyle choice).

ci: Understanding ourselves, our relationships and our wellbeing empowers us to thrive.
lo: The recognisable features of safe and healthy interactions (Form) ??? The connections between emotions, behaviour, learning, interactions and goals (Connection) ??? The tools and skills to stay safe online and in person (Safety) ??? The active role we play in achieving and maintaining our wellbeing (Wellbeing) ??? The strategies to achieve and maintain positive relationships (Relationships)

s.d: Students design and distribute a wellbeing survey using Microsoft Forms to gather anonymous data about their classmates' strategies for managing emotions, safe online interactions, and growth mindset. They analyse the collected responses to identify common themes and reflect on how understanding others? wellbeing supports thriving relationships and self-awareness.

Students design digital infographics or posters that visually map out their personal wellbeing strategies, safe online habits, and positive relationship tips. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create dynamic, inclusive visuals, and can collaborate in real time to produce a class-wide digital wellbeing gallery.

Students create a multimedia digital book that documents their personal journey through the 'Who We Are' unit, combining photos, audio reflections, and drawings to illustrate safe and healthy interactions, emotional connections, and strategies for wellbeing. They record and narrate scenarios about managing friendships, responding to challenges, and celebrating their strengths, producing a personalised digital portfolio that demonstrates their understanding of the central idea and lines of inquiry. This project helps students actively reflect on their relationships and wellbeing, making their learning visible and meaningful within the unit's themes.

Launch a collaborative Padlet wall where students anonymously post real-life dilemmas or questions about relationships, safety, and wellbeing. Peers respond with advice, strategies, or supportive messages, building a digital community of care and shared wisdom.

Students create interactive digital journals on Explain Everything to document their learning journey about wellbeing, emotions, and safe interactions. They combine voice recordings, drawings, photos, and text to reflect on personal goals and strategies for positive relationships. This dynamic tool empowers students to share their evolving understanding and receive real-time feedback from peers and teachers, deepening their self-awareness and connection to the unit?s central idea.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, tabletop 3D maze using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each section of the maze represents a real-life or online social scenario (e.g., resolving conflict, cyberbullying, supporting a friend, setting boundaries). Teams embed simple electronic components (e.g., Makey Makey, micro:bit, or conductive tape) to create interactive checkpoints?when a metal marble or touchpoint is triggered, a digital message or sound (programmed via Scratch or micro:bit) gives feedback or a tip related to safe and healthy interactions. Students use the Design Cycle to empathise (identify key wellbeing challenges), ideate (brainstorm maze paths and scenarios), prototype (build and wire the maze), test (navigate and refine), and reflect (share insights on managing emotions and friendships). The final product is a collaborative, hands-on learning tool that fuses tangible making with digital interactivity, reinforcing the unit's focus on wellbeing and growth mindset.

plannerText: This unit focuses on understanding safe and healthy interactions both online and in real life, strategies for managing friendships and emotions, and the importance of wellbeing and growth mindset. Students engage in activities such as cyber safety lessons, wellbeing poster creation, learner profile self-portraits, goal setting, and reflective Seesaw tasks to build self-awareness and positive relationships.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The recognisable features of safe and healthy interactions. That the concepts and skills learnt connect our emotions, behaviour, learning, interactions and goals in all aspects of our life. They have tools and skills to stay safe. We play an active role in achieving and maintaining our wellbeing. There are strategies we can learn and engage to support wellbeing. We have a role in achieving and maintaining positive relationships. There are strategies we can engage to manage the challenges and amplify the strengths of relationships.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The recognisable features of safe and healthy interactions; That the concepts and skills learnt connect our emotions, behaviour, learning, interactions and goals in all aspects of our life; They have tools and skills to stay safe; We play an active role in achieving and maintaining our wellbeing; There are strategies we can learn and engage to support wellbeing; We have a role in achieving and maintaining positive relationships; There are strategies we can engage to manage the challenges and amplify the strengths of relationships.

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Connection, Safety, Wellbeing, Relationships

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Use lessons from Cyber Safety throughout the year. Lesson 1: ROAR ? fingerprint activity. Students use a copy of the ROAR wellbeing poster to read through each statement, highlight key words, choose one word from each statement that is important to them, write their chosen word into giant individual letters/stencil, and put their fingerprint onto the poster to signify agreement with the Roar Approach. Lesson 2: IB Learner Profile ? students revise the Learner Profiles, then draw half of their portrait as normal and the other half filled with their strengths, hobbies, and what makes them unique. Lesson 3: Analysing the positive reward and behaviour chart as a class.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Cyber Safety lessons (ongoing)
- ROAR fingerprint activity and poster creation
- IB Learner Profile portrait drawing task
- Analysing positive reward and behaviour chart
- Discussion and creation of Essential Agreement using Rights and Responsibility Charter
- Grow Your Mind activities and podcasts (e.g., "You Can't Grow It Alone" podcast and student journal)
- Goal setting and executive functioning checklist (Term 2 and Semester 2)
- URStrong program activities
- Wellbeing Wednesday and other Grow Your Mind Seesaw tasks
- Seesaw reflection tasks after Camp Milana and Canberra Tour

ASSESSMENT: Measuring student ability to mirror explored strategies in their own words and responses at the end of a teaching period; evaluating ongoing understanding and application of strategies over time, even under stress; depth of reflection in end-of-unit Seesaw reflection; self and peer assessment of unit content, experiences, and reactions; group work sharing and discussion; self-reflection on executive skills and learning goals.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong emphasis on student agency: voice (whole class and small group discussion, sharing effective strategies), choice (what and how to share/respond), and ownership (structured opportunities to apply skills and knowledge in scenarios).
- Students encouraged to remain physically active outside PE classes and practice swimming skills with adult supervision.
- Integration of digital tools: Seesaw for reflection, portfolio, and assessment; Grow Your Mind and URStrong resources; Cyber Safety Project.
- Connections to class text "Can You See Me" for inclusion, belonging, and acceptance.
- Parents reported students shared knowledge and skills from the WWA unit at home, and multiple groups connected WWA to their exhibition unit of inquiry.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment are embedded throughout.
- Action opportunities include students applying strategies at home and in their exhibition inquiries.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to create a personal timeline, programming the car to travel along a path marked with significant events and memories from their lives. As Indi stops at each point, students share stories and artefacts, connecting past experiences to their present selves in a playful, hands-on way.

Students take photos of special objects or family artefacts and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, recording short oral stories about why these items are important. This brings personal histories to life and supports oral storytelling as seen in the unit's focus on sharing and sequencing memories.

Students create a digital timeline collage by arranging photos, drawings, and simple labels that represent their past and present selves, visually exploring the central idea of discovering their past to understand themselves today. They combine family photos, self-made artwork, and captions to sequence their personal stories in a colorful, engaging format that supports oral storytelling and reflection.

Document the inquiry process by having students upload photos of their drawings, timelines, and special objects, then record voice reflections about what they have learned about their past and family. Families can comment, fostering home-school connections and celebrating diverse histories.

Students create simple digital puppet shows to share their personal timelines by selecting characters and backgrounds that represent key moments from their past. They narrate their stories aloud while animating their puppets to sequence events, helping them express how their history shapes who they are today in a fun, age-appropriate way.

Students design and build a 'Memory Lane' train using recycled boxes, cardboard, and craft materials, where each train carriage represents a different moment or artefact from their family's past. They decorate each carriage with drawings, photos, or 3D objects (e.g., clay models, fabric scraps) to tell their story. Using Bee-Bots, students code simple journeys for their trains along a large floor map, stopping at each carriage to share their story with peers. The project follows the Design Cycle: students imagine their family's story, plan and sketch their train, build and decorate the carriages, program the Bee-Bot path, test and refine both physical and digital elements, and finally present their 'Memory Lane Story Train' to the class.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Discovering our past helps us know more about ourselves today' by sharing family stories, creating personal timelines, comparing past and present, and reflecting on personal artefacts and memories through drawing, oral storytelling, and sequencing activities.
humanVerifiedResetReason: Suggestion edited, swapped, regenerated or changed after human verification
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Discovering our past helps us know more about ourselves today.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Personal stories and histories; Reflecting on and recording our past; How we show our past in order

KEY CONCEPTS: Not specified.

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Teacher shares a baby photo as a provocation; students discuss past vs now and draw themselves as they are now. Students also bring in baby photos, talk about what has changed, and draw themselves as a baby vs now. Mystery bag with artefacts (baby shoe, old toy, photo) is used to prompt discussion about objects from the past.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Sharing family stories, photos, and artefacts
- Creating personal timelines (past to present)
- Exploring changes in their lives, school, and community
- Exploring where families come from using maps, cultures, and traditions
- Comparing childhood experiences across cultures
- Drawing "Me as a baby / Me now"
- Sequencing activities (then to now)
- Oral storytelling ("When I was little?")
- Sorting old vs new objects
- Drawing special memories
- Mystery bag activity with artefacts
- Matching objects to people (picture cards/discussion)
- Students bring a special item, draw it, and share why it is special
- Creating a "My Life in 3 Parts" timeline (baby, little, now)
- School walk to observe different areas and people (as part of local connections)

ASSESSMENT: 
- Students share their thinking from the inquiry through a Q&A video, which becomes a time capsule and evidence of their thinking around the central idea; this can be revealed to students at the PYP Exhibition.
- Teacher observations of class discussions, drawings, sequencing activities, and oral sharing circles.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include contributing to shared class displays (timelines, class museum), respecting and valuing different families and backgrounds, understanding that all families and stories are important, and sharing their own stories.
- Transdisciplinary connections include Maths (sequencing, patterns, measurement, space), English (recounts, mentor texts, oral storytelling), and global perspectives (family origins, cultural traditions).
- Technology integration: Use of video for Q&A and time capsule, potential for digital timelines or story sharing, and possible use of digital maps for exploring family origins.
- Emphasis on developing respect, identity, and belonging, and supporting communication and listening skills.
